## Sergeant Focus Areas
- **Complaint Recording Jurisdiction:** Mastering where complaints are recorded (precinct of occurrence rules, continuous event exceptions for homicide and Rape 1st Degree, Transit/Housing rules)
- **Investigation Thresholds:** Dollar amounts triggering Major Case Unit ($100K) and detective squad referrals ($5K/$10K Manhattan), victim age (60+), and repeat victimization (6 months)
- **Bias Incident Response:** The full escalation chain, the CO's duty to personally interview victims, 10-day reporting deadline, and that only the Hate Crime Unit CO determines bias classification
- **Corruption Reporting:** Absolute duty to report, Confidential ID Number process, failure to report as misconduct, and the interview sequence when a member is accused
- **Civilian Complaints:** Investigating Supervisor hierarchy (Platoon Commander first), reviewer rank requirement (one rank higher), and immediate Command Log entry for blank form requests
- **Missing Persons:** No minimum time limits, resident precinct recording, anyone can be a complainant, never refer to Missing Persons Squad directly
- **Alert Systems:** Silver Alert activation (Captain+ from Detective Bureau, 24-hour window, under-65 exception), Blue Alert criteria
- **Notification Matrix:** Which situations require Operations Unit notification, which go direct, and the IAB notification pattern (DOH, tax, postal, police impersonation)
- **Sex Crimes:** SVU notification for all sex crimes, victim's right to advocate, hospital walk-ins go directly to SVU without patrol response

---

# Section 207 — Complaint Reporting System

Covers P.G. 207-01, 207-02, 207-03, 207-04, 207-05, 207-09, 207-34

---

## P.G. 207-01: Complaint Reporting System

### Key Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Complaint** | A report of an alleged criminal offense or other matter requiring police attention |
| **Pick-Up Arrest** | An arrest of a person wanted by another command or agency |
| **Continuous Event** | A crime or series of related crimes spanning multiple locations or jurisdictions |
| **Incident** | A single event or occurrence reported in a complaint |
| **Offense** | A specific criminal charge; up to **10 offenses** may be recorded per incident |

### Where Complaints Are Recorded

The general rule: complaints are recorded in the **PRECINCT OF OCCURRENCE**.

#### Exceptions to Precinct of Occurrence

| Situation | Recorded At |
|-----------|-------------|
| Vice/Narcotics/Organized Crime | **Specialty Enforcement Division** |
| Vice exception: pick-up arrests | Precinct of **arrest** |
| Vice exception: found drugs **IN** building | Precinct of **occurrence** |
| Vice exception: found drugs 2 oz or less **NOT in** building | Precinct of **occurrence** |
| Missing NYC residents | Missing person's **resident** precinct |
| Piers/bulkheads/bodies in navigable waters | Precinct where **pier or bulkhead** is located |
| Lost property | Precinct where complainant **FIRST discovered** the loss |
| Overdue rental vehicles | Precinct where vehicle was **leased/rented** OR where it was **expected to be returned** |

### Continuous Event Rules

- **General rule:** Record at the precinct of the **INITIAL** offense.
- **Homicide exception:** Recorded where the **homicide occurred**, regardless of where the continuous event began.
- **Rape 1st Degree exception:** Recorded where the **rape occurred**, regardless of where the continuous event began.

### Special Bureau Jurisdiction

| Bureau | Rule |
|--------|------|
| **Housing Bureau** | Continuous event starting **on-development** remains Housing Bureau jurisdiction |
| **Transit Bureau** | Crime on a **moving train** is recorded at the **NEXT station where the train stops** |

### Forms

| Form | Number | Purpose |
|------|--------|---------|
| Complaint Report Worksheet | PD313-152A | Initial recording of complaint information |
| Complaint Report | PD313-152 | Official complaint record |
| Complaint Index Report | — | Indexing and cross-referencing |
| Omniform Complaint Revision | — | Amending/revising existing complaint reports |

---

## P.G. 207-02: Complaints Not Recorded on Complaint Report

Certain matters brought to the attention of the Department do not warrant a formal Complaint Report. These are handled through alternative documentation methods (e.g., Command Log entries, aided reports, or other administrative records) as appropriate to the nature of the complaint.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Ensure members understand which incidents require a formal Complaint Report and which are properly documented through alternative means. Misdocumentation can affect crime statistics and case tracking.

---

## P.G. 207-03: Voiding Complaint Reports

### When a Report May Be Voided

The **Desk Officer** determines if a complaint should be voided when it is:
- A **duplicate** of an existing report
- Filed at the **wrong precinct**
- For an event occurring **outside NYC**
- An event that **should have been recorded differently**

### Critical Distinction: VOIDED vs. UNFOUNDED

| Status | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| **VOIDED** | The report **should NOT have been prepared** in the first place (procedural error) |
| **UNFOUNDED** | The report **was properly prepared**, but subsequent investigation reveals that **no offense actually occurred** |

> **Exam Alert:** This distinction is heavily tested. A voided report is a clerical/procedural correction. An unfounded case reflects an investigative finding that no crime took place.

---

## P.G. 207-04: Reporting of Complaints Occurring in Another Command

When a complaint is reported at a precinct other than the precinct of occurrence:
- The receiving command documents the complaint and ensures the information is transmitted to the **precinct of occurrence** for proper recording.
- The precinct of occurrence retains responsibility for the complaint and any subsequent investigation.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Supervisors must ensure that complaints taken for another command are promptly and accurately forwarded. Delays can compromise investigations.

---

## P.G. 207-05: Duplicate Copies of Complaint Reports

Procedures govern when and how duplicate copies of complaint reports are prepared and distributed. Duplicate copies ensure that all relevant commands, squads, and units have the information necessary to conduct follow-up investigations or provide support.

---

## P.G. 207-09: Follow-Up Investigations of Complaints Already Recorded

When additional information is obtained on a previously recorded complaint:
- The follow-up investigation is coordinated with the detective squad or specialized unit assigned to the case.
- Updates are documented through the Omniform Complaint Revision process.
- The original complaint report's status (active, closed, unfounded) is updated accordingly.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisors should ensure that officers conducting follow-up canvasses or receiving new information properly route it to the assigned investigative unit rather than creating new complaint reports.

---

## P.G. 207-34: Complaint File

The Complaint File is the centralized repository for all complaint-related documentation within a command. It includes:
- Original complaint reports and worksheets
- Follow-up investigation notes
- Correspondence related to cases
- Status updates and dispositions

> **Sergeant Focus:** Supervisors are responsible for ensuring the complaint file is maintained in an orderly and up-to-date fashion, facilitating case review and audits.

---

## Supervisor Quick Reference

### Complaint Recording Decision Tree

```
Is it a criminal offense or police matter?
├── NO → Document via Command Log or other appropriate record (207-02)
└── YES → Prepare Complaint Report
    ├── Is it Vice/Narcotics/Organized Crime?
    │   ├── YES → Specialty Enforcement Division (with exceptions)
    │   └── NO → Continue
    ├── Is it a missing NYC resident?
    │   ├── YES → Resident precinct
    │   └── NO → Continue
    ├── Is it a continuous event?
    │   ├── YES → Precinct of INITIAL offense
    │   │   ├── UNLESS homicide → where homicide occurred
    │   │   └── UNLESS Rape 1st → where rape occurred
    │   └── NO → Precinct of OCCURRENCE
    └── Lost property? → Where loss FIRST discovered
```

---

# Section 207 — Corruption Allegations and Civilian Complaints

Covers P.G. 207-21, 207-22, 207-27, 207-28

---

## P.G. 207-21: Allegations of Corruption and Other Misconduct Against Members of the Service

*Critical procedure — tested heavily on the Sergeant exam.*

### Absolute Duty to Report

**ALL members of the service have an ABSOLUTE DUTY to report corruption and misconduct.**

This is not discretionary. Every member who becomes aware of corruption or other misconduct must report it.

### How to Report

- Report to the **IAB Command Center** (operates **24 hours**)
- Reports **can be made anonymously**
- When reporting anonymously, the member receives a **Confidential ID Number**

### Confidential ID Number

- Obtaining a Confidential ID Number **satisfies the reporting responsibility**
- The member need not identify themselves — the Confidential ID Number serves as proof that the report was made

### Failure to Report

- **FAILURE TO REPORT** corruption or misconduct is **itself serious misconduct**
- Conduct undertaken to **cover up corruption** constitutes **obstruction of justice**

> **Exam Alert:** Three critical points: (1) Absolute duty — no exceptions, (2) Confidential ID Number satisfies the duty, (3) Failure to report is itself misconduct.

### When an Allegation Is Made Against You

If a member of the service has an allegation made **against them**, the procedure is:

1. The accused member **requests a supervisor to respond**
2. The supervisor **interviews the complainant**
3. The supervisor **confers with IAB**
4. **BEFORE** interviewing the accused member

> **Exam Alert:** The sequence matters. The supervisor interviews the complainant and contacts IAB **BEFORE** interviewing the member accused of misconduct.

---

## P.G. 207-22: Allegations of Corruption Against City Employees

When allegations of corruption are made against city employees (non-NYPD):
- The receiving member documents the allegation
- The information is routed to IAB and the appropriate Inspector General for the relevant city agency
- Procedures parallel those for members of the service, but the investigative authority may differ depending on the agency involved

---

## P.G. 207-27: Civilian Complaints — Witness Statement

When a civilian complaint is received, witness statements are a critical component of the investigation:
- Statements from witnesses are obtained to corroborate or refute the allegation
- The investigating supervisor ensures that all available witnesses are interviewed
- Witness statements are documented and included in the complaint file

---

## P.G. 207-28: Processing Civilian Complaints

*7 pages in the Patrol Guide — critical for supervisors.*

### Investigating Supervisor

The **Investigating Supervisor** is defined by a specific hierarchy:

| Priority | Title |
|----------|-------|
| 1st | **Platoon Commander** |
| 2nd | **Special Operations Lieutenant** |
| 3rd | **Integrity Control Officer (ICO)** |
| If all unavailable | **CO or Duty Captain** |

### Reviewer Rank Requirement

The **Supervisor Reviewing a Civilian Complaint** must be at least **ONE RANK HIGHER** than the member who is the subject of the complaint.

### Where Complaints Can Be Made

Civilian complaints can be made at **ANY** of the following locations:
- Patrol precinct
- Police Service Area (PSA)
- Transit district
- Traffic unit
- Internal Affairs Bureau (IAB)
- Any Department office

### Methods of Filing a Complaint

| Method | Notes |
|--------|-------|
| **In person** | Complainant appears and wishes to file immediately |
| **By mail** | Written complaint received through postal mail |
| **By telephone** | Complaint received via phone call |
| **In person, not wanting to file immediately** | Complainant appears but is not ready to file at that time |

### Request for Blank Complaint Form

When someone requests a blank civilian complaint form:
1. The **Desk Officer** is advised
2. A **Command Log entry** is made **IMMEDIATELY**

> **Exam Alert:** The immediate Command Log entry when someone merely requests a blank form is a testable detail. It applies even if the person does not ultimately file a complaint.

---

## Supervisor Quick Reference — Corruption and Civilian Complaints

### Corruption Report Flow

```
Member becomes aware of corruption/misconduct
   ↓
Report to IAB Command Center (24 hours)
   ↓
Can be anonymous → receive Confidential ID Number
   ↓
Confidential ID Number = duty satisfied
```

### Allegation Against a Member Flow

```
Allegation made against member
   ↓
Member requests supervisor to respond
   ↓
Supervisor interviews COMPLAINANT
   ↓
Supervisor confers with IAB
   ↓
THEN (and only then) supervisor interviews the MEMBER
```

### Investigating Supervisor Hierarchy

```
Platoon Commander
   ↓ (unavailable)
Special Operations Lieutenant
   ↓ (unavailable)
Integrity Control Officer (ICO)
   ↓ (unavailable)
CO or Duty Captain
```

---

# Section 207 — Investigation Procedures

Covers P.G. 207-07, 207-08, 207-10, 207-29, 207-33

---

## P.G. 207-07: Preliminary Investigation of Complaints (Other Than Vice/Narcotics)

*7 pages in the Patrol Guide — one of the most critical procedures for supervisors.*

### Key Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Active Case** | A case with leads still being pursued or investigative steps remaining |
| **Closed Case** | A case where all reasonable investigative steps have been completed |
| **Unfounded Case** | A case where investigation determines that no offense actually occurred |

### Crime Scene Preservation

**Do NOT disturb evidence at the crime scene.** This is a fundamental responsibility for all responding members and supervisors.

### Patrol Supervisor Notification Requirements

The Patrol Supervisor must **PROMPTLY** report to the following specialized units:

| Unit to Notify | Circumstances |
|----------------|---------------|
| **Major Case Unit** | Bank burglary or robbery |
| **Major Case Unit** | Truck contents valued over **$100,000** |
| **Major Case Unit** | Commercial burglary loss over **$100,000** |
| **Major Case Unit** | Art theft |
| **Joint Violent Crimes Task Force (JVCTF)** | Armed bank robberies |
| **Highway District** | Fatal or serious injury collisions |
| **Special Victims Unit (SVU)** | ALL sex crimes |
| **Special Victims Unit (SVU)** | Child abuse involving victims under 11 |
| **Special Victims Unit (SVU)** | Human trafficking |

> **Exam Alert:** Memorize the **$100,000** threshold for Major Case Unit notifications (truck contents AND commercial burglary).

### When to Refer to Detective Squad

Refer a case to the detective squad when any of the following apply:

| Condition | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| Serious physical injury | Any complaint involving serious physical injury to a victim |
| Robbery with firearm | Or robbery where victim is **60 years or older** |
| Burglary with person present | Or burglary loss exceeding **$5,000** (or **$10,000 in Manhattan**) |
| Unique modus operandi | Crime committed with a distinctive or unusual method |
| Repeat victimization | Same victim targeted **within 6 months** |
| Perpetrator may be identified | Leads exist that could identify the suspect |
| Similar crimes in vicinity | Pattern of related criminal activity |
| Police impersonation | Any crime involving impersonation of a police officer |
| Identity theft | Any complaint involving identity theft |
| Felonious assault on retail workers | Assaults on retail employees in the course of their employment |

> **Exam Alert:** The burglary threshold differs by borough: **$5,000** citywide, but **$10,000 in Manhattan**. This Manhattan exception is frequently tested.

---

## P.G. 207-08: Preliminary Investigation of Vice Related, Narcotics, or Organized Crime Complaints

Vice, narcotics, and organized crime complaints follow a separate investigative track routed through the **Specialty Enforcement Division**. The preliminary investigation procedures differ from standard complaints:

- Members receiving such complaints ensure initial documentation and notify the appropriate specialized unit.
- Patrol supervisors coordinate with vice/narcotics units for proper case handling.
- Exceptions for recording at the precinct of occurrence (pick-up arrests, found drugs) still apply per P.G. 207-01.

---

## P.G. 207-10: Bias Motivated Incidents

*5 pages in the Patrol Guide — high priority for the Sergeant exam.*

### Definition

A **Bias Incident** is any offense or attempted offense motivated in whole or in part by the offender's bias based on:
- Race
- Religion
- Ethnicity
- Gender
- Age
- Disability
- Sexual orientation (including **LGBTQIA+**)

### Response Chain

The response chain follows a specific escalation path:

```
1. MOS evaluates the incident for possible bias motivation
   ↓
2. MOS requests PATROL SUPERVISOR to respond
   ↓
3. Patrol Supervisor requests CO/DUTY CAPTAIN
   ↓
4. CO/Duty Captain notifies OPERATIONS UNIT
   ↓
5. Operations Unit issues a BIAS INCIDENT LOG NUMBER
```

### Commanding Officer / Duty Captain Responsibilities

Upon notification of a potential bias incident, the CO or Duty Captain must:

1. **Request Enhanced Crime Team (ECT)** to respond
2. **Request detective squad** to respond
3. **Prepare an UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT** (PD370-152)

### Who Determines Bias Classification

> **Only the Commanding Officer of the Hate Crime Unit** determines whether an incident IS or IS NOT a bias incident, after conferral with the **Chief of Detectives**.

No other rank or unit makes this final determination.

### Commanding Officer of the Command Concerned

- Must **VISIT** the scene or location
- Must **PERSONALLY INTERVIEW** the victims
- This is a mandatory, non-delegable responsibility

### Reporting Timeline

- Reports are due within **TEN (10) DAYS** of the incident

> **Exam Alert:** Three key testable points: (1) Only the Hate Crime Unit CO determines bias classification, (2) the CO of the command concerned must personally interview victims, and (3) the 10-day reporting deadline.

---

## P.G. 207-29: Investigation of Carjackings

Carjacking investigations involve a coordinated response between patrol, the detective squad, and specialized units. Key supervisor responsibilities include:

- Ensuring immediate notification of the detective squad
- Coordinating a canvass of the area
- Broadcasting vehicle and suspect descriptions
- Ensuring the stolen vehicle alarm is transmitted promptly
- Coordinating with neighboring commands when the crime occurs near precinct boundaries

---

## P.G. 207-33: Complaints Involving Sex Crimes

*4 pages in the Patrol Guide — critical for supervisors.*

### Core Requirement

The Patrol Supervisor must **PROMPTLY** report **ALL sex crimes** to the **Special Victims Unit (SVU)**.

### Victim Rights

- The victim is entitled to have a **sexual assault advocate** present during any interview.
- This right must be communicated to the victim.

### Hospital Walk-In Sex Crime Complaints

When a sex crime victim presents at a hospital (walk-in):
- The **911 dispatcher** refers the complaint **DIRECTLY to SVU**.
- **Patrol is NOT directed to respond** to the hospital for these complaints.
- SVU handles hospital walk-in sex crime complaints from initial response onward.

### SVU as Central Repository

SVU serves as the **central repository** for all sex crime information across the Department. All sex crime data is routed through and maintained by SVU.

> **Exam Alert:** The hospital walk-in procedure is a common test question. Remember: 911 sends it directly to SVU, patrol does NOT respond.

---

## Supervisor Quick Reference — Investigation Thresholds

| Threshold | Triggers |
|-----------|----------|
| **$100,000** | Major Case Unit: truck contents, commercial burglary |
| **$10,000** | Detective squad referral: burglary in **Manhattan** |
| **$5,000** | Detective squad referral: burglary **citywide** (except Manhattan) |
| **60+ years old** | Detective squad referral: robbery victim age |
| **6 months** | Detective squad referral: same victim re-victimized within this period |
| **Under 11** | SVU notification: child abuse victim age |
| **10 days** | Bias incident report deadline |

---

# Section 207 — Missing Persons and Alert Systems

Covers P.G. 207-23, 207-31, 207-39

---

## P.G. 207-23: Missing Persons

*7 pages in the Patrol Guide — high priority for the Sergeant exam.*

### No Minimum Time Requirement

There are **NO MINIMUM TIME LIMITS** before accepting a missing person report. A report must be taken immediately upon request.

> **Exam Alert:** This is one of the most commonly tested points. There is NO waiting period (no "24-hour rule" or any other delay). Accept the report immediately.

### Where Missing Person Reports Are Recorded

Missing person reports are recorded at the missing person's **RESIDENT precinct**.

### Command Responsibility

The **Commanding Officer of the residence precinct** is **ULTIMATELY RESPONSIBLE** for the missing person investigation.

### Missing Persons from Outside NYC

Reports for persons missing from **outside New York City** will **NOT be accepted**. The complainant should be directed to their **local police department**.

### Do Not Refer to Missing Persons Squad

Under **NO circumstances** should the complainant be referred directly to the Missing Persons Squad. The complaint is always taken at the precinct level first.

### Who Can Be a Complainant

The **complainant is NOT limited to family members**. Any of the following may report a person missing:
- Guardian
- School representative
- Hospital administrator
- Roommate
- Any other person with knowledge of the missing person's circumstances

> **Sergeant Focus:** Ensure desk officers and patrol members do not turn away non-family complainants. Anyone with a legitimate concern may file a missing person report.

---

## P.G. 207-31: Silver Alert System

*4 pages in the Patrol Guide.*

### Definition — Vulnerable Senior

A **vulnerable senior** for Silver Alert purposes is a person who is:
- **65 years of age or older** with **dementia** (Alzheimer's disease or similar condition)
- Missing under circumstances indicating **imminent danger** to their health or safety

### Who Can Activate a Silver Alert

**ONLY a Captain or above from the Detective Bureau** may activate a Silver Alert.

No lower rank and no unit outside the Detective Bureau has this authority.

### Timing Requirement

A Silver Alert must be issued within **24 HOURS** of the determination that the person qualifies for the alert.

### Tactical Consideration

The most prudent approach is to activate the Silver Alert **AFTER** patrol and field resources have been exhausted. The alert system supplements — rather than replaces — immediate search efforts.

### Under-65 Exception

A Silver Alert can **also be activated for persons under 65** who have dementia, if the **Detective Zone Commanding Officer** reasonably believes activation will be beneficial to the investigation.

> **Exam Alert:** Key testable points: (1) Captain or above from Detective Bureau activates, (2) 24-hour window, (3) under-65 exception exists with Detective Zone CO approval.

---

## P.G. 207-39: Blue Alert System

### Purpose

The Blue Alert System is activated when a **law enforcement officer** has been:
- **Killed** in the line of duty, OR
- **Seriously injured** in the line of duty, OR
- **Missing** in the line of duty

AND the **suspect has not been apprehended**.

### Activation

The Blue Alert disseminates critical information to the public and law enforcement to assist in locating the suspect. It is reserved for situations where the threat to officer safety is ongoing.

---

## Supervisor Quick Reference — Missing Persons Decision Tree

```
Person reported missing
├── Is the missing person a NYC resident?
│   ├── NO → Do NOT accept report; direct to local police
│   └── YES → Accept IMMEDIATELY (no time limit)
│       ├── Record at RESIDENT precinct
│       ├── CO of residence precinct is ULTIMATELY responsible
│       └── Do NOT refer complainant to Missing Persons Squad
│
├── Is the complainant a family member?
│   └── Does NOT matter — anyone can be a complainant
│
└── Is this a vulnerable senior (65+ with dementia)?
    ├── YES → Silver Alert eligible
    │   └── Captain+ from Detective Bureau activates within 24 hours
    └── NO, but under-65 with dementia?
        └── Detective Zone CO may authorize Silver Alert if beneficial
```

## Alert Systems Comparison

| Feature | Silver Alert | Blue Alert |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| **Subject** | Vulnerable senior (65+ with dementia) | Law enforcement officer |
| **Trigger** | Missing under dangerous circumstances | Killed, seriously injured, or missing; suspect at large |
| **Who activates** | Captain+ from Detective Bureau | Department-level activation |
| **Time requirement** | Within 24 hours of determination | Immediate upon qualifying event |
| **Under-65 exception** | Yes, with Detective Zone CO approval | N/A |

---

# Section 207 — Notification Requirements

Covers P.G. 207-06, 207-24

---

## P.G. 207-06: Notifications

*3 pages in the Patrol Guide — critical notification matrix for supervisors.*

### Desk Officer Notifications to Operations Unit

The Desk Officer must notify the **Operations Unit** for the following situations:

| Situation | Additional Notification |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Counterfeit U.S. obligations | **Secret Service** |
| DOH employee crime or impersonation | **Inspector General, DOH** + **IAB** |
| Tax official crimes | **Inspector General, Dept. of Finance** + **IAB** |
| Postal property or employee crimes | **Postal Inspector** + **IAB** |
| Contraband weapons | **ATF (Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives)** |
| Product tampering | **New York State Police** |
| Home invasion robbery | **Detective Bureau Wheel** |

### Direct Notifications (No Operations Unit)

| Situation | Notify Directly |
|-----------|----------------|
| Child abuse (victim under 18) | **New York State Child Abuse Register** |
| Police impersonation | **IAB Command Center** |
| Private carting / wholesale markets / gambling boats | **Business Integrity Commission** (via DB Wheel) |

### Specialized Crime Notifications

| Situation | Notify |
|-----------|--------|
| ATM larceny | **Grand Larceny Squad** (via email) |
| Skimmer device / financial information recording device | **Financial Crimes Task Force** |
| Sexual assault of prisoner in custody | **Criminal Justice Bureau** |

> **Exam Alert:** The notification matrix is heavily tested. Focus on which situations require Operations Unit notification versus direct notification, and which require IAB involvement (DOH, tax officials, postal matters all require IAB).

### IAB Notification Pattern

Notice that **three** categories require both Operations Unit AND IAB notification:
1. DOH employee/impersonation
2. Tax official crimes
3. Postal property/employee crimes

Police impersonation goes **directly** to IAB (no Operations Unit).

---

## P.G. 207-24: Notifications from Caseworkers (Adult Protective Services / Dept. for the Aging)

When notifications are received from caseworkers of Adult Protective Services or the Department for the Aging:
- The information is documented and appropriate action is taken
- These notifications often involve vulnerable adults who may be victims of abuse, neglect, or exploitation
- Patrol supervisors ensure that responding officers conduct welfare checks and document findings

> **Sergeant Focus:** These notifications frequently involve elderly or incapacitated persons. Ensure responding members are sensitive to the circumstances and thorough in documentation.

---

## Supervisor Quick Reference — Notification Matrix

### Notifications Requiring Operations Unit

```
Counterfeit U.S. obligations ────────→ Operations Unit + Secret Service
DOH employee/impersonation ──────────→ Operations Unit + IG DOH + IAB
Tax official crimes ─────────────────→ Operations Unit + IG Finance + IAB
Postal property/employee crimes ─────→ Operations Unit + Postal Inspector + IAB
Contraband weapons ──────────────────→ Operations Unit + ATF
Product tampering ───────────────────→ Operations Unit + NYS Police
Home invasion robbery ───────────────→ Operations Unit + DB Wheel
```

### Direct Notifications (Bypass Operations Unit)

```
Child abuse (under 18) ──────────────→ NYS Child Abuse Register
Police impersonation ────────────────→ IAB Command Center
Private carting/wholesale/gambling ──→ Business Integrity Commission (via DB Wheel)
ATM larceny ─────────────────────────→ Grand Larceny Squad (email)
Skimmer device ──────────────────────→ Financial Crimes Task Force
Sexual assault of prisoner ──────────→ Criminal Justice Bureau
```

### Memory Aid: When Does IAB Get Notified?

| Situation | IAB? |
|-----------|------|
| DOH employee/impersonation | YES |
| Tax official crimes | YES |
| Postal property/employee | YES |
| Police impersonation | YES (direct) |
| All other notification categories | NO |

---

# Section 207 — Key Terms, Forms, and Abbreviations

---

## Core Complaint Definitions (P.G. 207-01)

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Complaint** | A report of an alleged criminal offense or other matter requiring police attention | P.G. 207-01 |
| **Pick-Up Arrest** | An arrest of a person wanted by another command or agency | P.G. 207-01 |
| **Continuous Event** | A crime or series of related crimes spanning multiple locations or jurisdictions | P.G. 207-01 |
| **Incident** | A single event or occurrence reported in a complaint | P.G. 207-01 |
| **Offense** | A specific criminal charge; up to 10 may be recorded per incident | P.G. 207-01 |

## Case Status Definitions (P.G. 207-07)

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Active Case** | A case with leads still being pursued or investigative steps remaining | P.G. 207-07 |
| **Closed Case** | A case where all reasonable investigative steps have been completed | P.G. 207-07 |
| **Unfounded Case** | Investigation determines that no offense actually occurred (report was properly prepared) | P.G. 207-07 |

## Report Status Distinction (P.G. 207-03)

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Voided** | The report should NOT have been prepared in the first place (procedural error) | P.G. 207-03 |
| **Unfounded** | The report was properly prepared, but no offense actually occurred (investigative finding) | P.G. 207-03, 207-07 |

## Bias and Discrimination Terms (P.G. 207-10, 207-36)

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Bias Incident** | An offense motivated by bias based on race, religion, ethnicity, gender, age, disability, or sexual orientation (including LGBTQIA+) | P.G. 207-10 |
| **Disability** | A physical, mental, or medical impairment that substantially limits major life activities | P.G. 207-36 |

## Alert System Terms (P.G. 207-31, 207-39)

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Vulnerable Senior** | A person 65+ with dementia (Alzheimer's or similar), missing under circumstances indicating imminent danger | P.G. 207-31 |
| **Silver Alert** | Alert system for missing vulnerable seniors; may also apply to under-65 with dementia per Detective Zone CO | P.G. 207-31 |
| **Blue Alert** | Alert system activated when a law enforcement officer is killed, seriously injured, or missing in the line of duty and the suspect is at large | P.G. 207-39 |

## Corruption and Misconduct Terms (P.G. 207-21, 207-22)

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Corruption/Other Misconduct** | Illegal or improper conduct by a member of the service; all members have an absolute duty to report | P.G. 207-21 |
| **Confidential ID Number** | Number issued to anonymous reporters of corruption; obtaining it satisfies the reporting duty | P.G. 207-21 |

## Civilian Complaint Terms (P.G. 207-27, 207-28)

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Investigating Supervisor** | The supervisor who investigates a civilian complaint: Platoon Commander, Special Ops Lt., ICO, or CO/Duty Captain | P.G. 207-28 |
| **Supervisor Reviewing Civilian Complaint** | Must be at least one rank higher than the member receiving the complaint | P.G. 207-28 |

## Immigration-Related Terms (P.G. 207-37)

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **U Visa** | A visa for victims of qualifying criminal activity who have suffered substantial abuse and are helpful to law enforcement | P.G. 207-37 |
| **Qualifying Criminal Activity** | Specified crimes that make a victim eligible for U Visa certification | P.G. 207-37 |
| **Certifying Official** | The Department official authorized to sign U Visa certification forms | P.G. 207-37 |

---

## Forms

| Form Name | Form Number | Purpose | Reference |
|-----------|-------------|---------|-----------|
| Complaint Report Worksheet | PD313-152A | Initial recording of complaint information | P.G. 207-01 |
| Complaint Report | PD313-152 | Official complaint record | P.G. 207-01 |
| Civilian Complaint Form | PD313-154 | Recording civilian complaints against members | P.G. 207-28 |
| Unusual Occurrence Report | PD370-152 | Documenting unusual occurrences including bias incidents | P.G. 207-10 |
| Complaint Index Report | — | Indexing and cross-referencing complaints | P.G. 207-01 |
| Omniform Complaint Revision | — | Amending/revising existing complaint reports | P.G. 207-01 |

---

## Units and Abbreviations

| Abbreviation | Full Name | Context |
|-------------|-----------|---------|
| **SVU** | Special Victims Unit | All sex crimes, child abuse (under 11), human trafficking |
| **IAB** | Internal Affairs Bureau | Corruption, misconduct, police impersonation |
| **ECT** | Enhanced Crime Team | Responds to bias incidents |
| **Hate Crime Unit** | Hate Crime Unit (under Detective Bureau) | Only unit that determines bias classification |
| **Major Case Unit** | Major Case Unit | Bank robberies/burglaries, truck contents >$100K, commercial burglary >$100K, art theft |
| **JVCTF** | Joint Violent Crimes Task Force | Armed bank robberies |
| **Financial Crimes Task Force** | Financial Crimes Task Force | Skimmer devices, financial information recording |
| **Missing Persons Squad** | Missing Persons Squad | Coordinates missing person investigations (complainants NOT referred directly) |
| **ATF** | Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives | Contraband weapons notification |
| **DB Wheel** | Detective Bureau Wheel | Routing mechanism for detective notifications |
| **ICO** | Integrity Control Officer | Third in hierarchy for investigating civilian complaints |

---

# Section 207 — Review Questions

20 multiple-choice questions covering key Sergeant exam topics from Section 207.

---

### Question 1
**A burglary occurs on a subway train as it travels between two precincts. At which location should the complaint be recorded?**

A. The precinct where the train originated
B. The next station where the train stops
C. The precinct where the victim resides
D. The precinct covering the midpoint of the route

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 207-01, complaints occurring on a moving train are recorded at the NEXT station where the train stops. This is a Transit Bureau jurisdiction rule.
</details>

---

### Question 2
**A continuous event begins with a kidnapping in the 75th Precinct, moves through the 77th Precinct, and the victim is murdered in the 84th Precinct. Where is the complaint recorded?**

A. The 75th Precinct, where the continuous event began
B. The 77th Precinct, where the event passed through
C. The 84th Precinct, where the homicide occurred
D. The precinct of the victim's residence

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 207-01, while continuous events are generally recorded at the precinct of the INITIAL offense, homicide is an exception — it is recorded where the homicide occurred (84th Precinct).
</details>

---

### Question 3
**A continuous event involves a robbery in the 40th Precinct followed by a Rape 1st Degree in the 42nd Precinct. Where is the complaint recorded?**

A. The 40th Precinct, where the initial offense occurred
B. The 42nd Precinct, where the rape occurred
C. Both precincts prepare separate complaint reports
D. The Special Victims Unit records the complaint

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 207-01, Rape 1st Degree is an exception to the continuous event rule. Like homicide, it is recorded where the rape occurred, regardless of where the continuous event began.
</details>

---

### Question 4
**A complainant reports losing her wallet. She believes she last had it at a restaurant in the 19th Precinct but first realized it was missing when she arrived home in the 23rd Precinct. Where is the complaint recorded?**

A. The 19th Precinct, where she last had the wallet
B. The 23rd Precinct, where she first discovered the loss
C. Either precinct, at the complainant's choice
D. The precinct nearest to the restaurant

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 207-01, lost property complaints are recorded at the precinct where the complainant FIRST DISCOVERED the loss, which is the 23rd Precinct.
</details>

---

### Question 5
**A desk officer reviews a complaint report and determines that an identical report was already filed at the same precinct for the same incident. What is the proper disposition?**

A. Mark the report as unfounded
B. Void the report
C. Forward both reports to the detective squad
D. Combine both reports into one

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 207-03, the Desk Officer should void the report. A duplicate report is one of the grounds for voiding — the report should not have been prepared because it already exists. This is distinct from unfounded, which means investigation found no crime occurred.
</details>

---

### Question 6
**What is the key distinction between a VOIDED complaint report and an UNFOUNDED complaint report?**

A. Voided means the crime was not serious enough; unfounded means no crime occurred
B. Voided means the report should not have been prepared; unfounded means investigation found no offense occurred
C. Voided requires supervisor approval; unfounded requires detective squad approval
D. Voided applies to misdemeanors; unfounded applies to felonies

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 207-03, VOIDED means the report should NOT have been prepared in the first place (e.g., duplicate, wrong precinct). UNFOUNDED means the report was properly prepared, but subsequent investigation determined that no offense actually occurred.
</details>

---

### Question 7
**A patrol supervisor responds to a commercial burglary in Brooklyn where the loss is estimated at $7,000. What is the proper action regarding detective squad notification?**

A. Do not refer to the detective squad; the loss is below the threshold
B. Refer to the detective squad; the loss exceeds $5,000
C. Refer to the Major Case Unit; commercial burglary requires their notification
D. Refer to the detective squad only if the loss exceeds $10,000

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 207-07, a burglary with a loss exceeding $5,000 should be referred to the detective squad. The $10,000 threshold applies only to Manhattan. Since this occurred in Brooklyn, the $5,000 threshold applies.
</details>

---

### Question 8
**A patrol supervisor responds to a commercial burglary in Manhattan where the estimated loss is $8,000. Should this be referred to the detective squad?**

A. Yes, the loss exceeds the $5,000 citywide threshold
B. No, in Manhattan the threshold is $10,000
C. Yes, all commercial burglaries are referred regardless of loss amount
D. No, commercial burglaries are only referred to the Major Case Unit

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 207-07, the detective squad referral threshold for burglary in Manhattan is $10,000, not $5,000. Since the loss of $8,000 is below the Manhattan threshold, this does not meet the dollar-amount criterion for referral (though other factors such as person present or unique MO could still warrant referral).
</details>

---

### Question 9
**Which of the following situations requires the patrol supervisor to notify the Major Case Unit?**

A. A robbery where the victim is 65 years old
B. A truck hijacking where the contents are valued at $80,000
C. A bank burglary
D. A burglary where a person was present in the building

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 207-07, the Major Case Unit must be notified for bank burglary/robbery, truck contents over $100,000, commercial burglary over $100,000, and art theft. A bank burglary qualifies. The truck hijacking at $80,000 is below the $100,000 threshold. The elderly victim robbery and person-present burglary are detective squad referrals, not Major Case Unit.
</details>

---

### Question 10
**In a bias motivated incident, who makes the final determination as to whether the incident IS or IS NOT a bias incident?**

A. The patrol supervisor who responds to the scene
B. The Commanding Officer of the precinct where the incident occurred
C. The Commanding Officer of the Hate Crime Unit, after conferral with the Chief of Detectives
D. The Operations Unit, after reviewing the Unusual Occurrence Report

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 207-10, ONLY the Commanding Officer of the Hate Crime Unit determines whether an incident is or is not a bias incident, after conferral with the Chief of Detectives. No other rank or unit makes this determination.
</details>

---

### Question 11
**Upon notification of a potential bias incident, the Commanding Officer of the command concerned must take what specific action regarding the victims?**

A. Assign a detective to interview the victims within 48 hours
B. Personally visit and interview the victims
C. Ensure a patrol supervisor interviews the victims before end of tour
D. Direct the Enhanced Crime Team to conduct victim interviews

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 207-10, the CO of the command concerned must VISIT and PERSONALLY INTERVIEW the victims. This is a mandatory, non-delegable responsibility.
</details>

---

### Question 12
**Reports related to bias motivated incidents must be submitted within what time frame?**

A. 48 hours
B. 5 days
C. 10 days
D. 30 days

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 207-10, reports related to bias incidents are due within TEN (10) DAYS of the incident.
</details>

---

### Question 13
**A member of the service witnesses another member engaging in corrupt activity. The witnessing member fears retaliation and does not want to be identified. What satisfies the member's reporting obligation?**

A. Reporting anonymously to the local precinct desk officer
B. Documenting the incident in the member's personal memo book
C. Obtaining a Confidential ID Number from the IAB Command Center
D. Reporting to a patrol supervisor who will relay the information

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 207-21, members can report corruption anonymously to the IAB Command Center (24 hours) and receive a Confidential ID Number. Obtaining this number satisfies the member's reporting responsibility.
</details>

---

### Question 14
**A member of the service becomes aware of corruption but decides not to report it. What is the consequence?**

A. No consequence unless the member personally participated in the corruption
B. The member may face administrative counseling
C. Failure to report is itself considered serious misconduct
D. The member faces consequences only if the corruption is later discovered

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 207-21, FAILURE TO REPORT corruption or misconduct is itself serious misconduct. All members have an ABSOLUTE DUTY to report. Additionally, conduct to cover up corruption constitutes obstruction of justice.
</details>

---

### Question 15
**A civilian makes an allegation of misconduct against an officer. The officer's supervisor responds. What must the supervisor do BEFORE interviewing the accused member?**

A. Notify the Commanding Officer and obtain permission to investigate
B. Interview the complainant and confer with IAB
C. Obtain a written statement from the accused member
D. Review the member's disciplinary history

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 207-21, when an allegation is made against a member, the supervisor interviews the complainant and confers with IAB BEFORE interviewing the accused member. The sequence is critical.
</details>

---

### Question 16
**Who is the first choice as the "Investigating Supervisor" for a civilian complaint under P.G. 207-28?**

A. The Integrity Control Officer (ICO)
B. The Special Operations Lieutenant
C. The Platoon Commander
D. The Commanding Officer

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 207-28, the hierarchy for Investigating Supervisor is: (1) Platoon Commander, (2) Special Operations Lieutenant, (3) ICO. The CO or Duty Captain acts only if all three are unavailable.
</details>

---

### Question 17
**A woman arrives at the precinct to report her neighbor missing. The neighbor is not a family member. The desk officer should:**

A. Accept the report — the complainant need not be a family member
B. Refuse the report — only family members can file missing person reports
C. Accept the report but note that it is from a non-family member and may be unreliable
D. Direct the woman to contact the Missing Persons Squad directly

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**A.** Per P.G. 207-23, the complainant is NOT limited to family members. A guardian, school representative, hospital administrator, roommate, or any other person may report someone missing. Additionally, under NO circumstances should the complainant be referred directly to the Missing Persons Squad.
</details>

---

### Question 18
**A family calls the precinct at 10 PM to report their elderly father, who has Alzheimer's disease, as missing. He left the house two hours ago. The desk officer states they must wait 24 hours before filing a report. Is this correct?**

A. Yes, missing person reports require a 24-hour waiting period
B. Yes, but only for adults — children can be reported immediately
C. No, there are no minimum time limits before accepting a missing person report
D. No, but only because the person has a medical condition

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 207-23, there are NO MINIMUM TIME LIMITS before accepting a missing person report. The report must be taken immediately, regardless of the person's age or medical condition. The 24-hour waiting period is a myth that has no basis in NYPD procedure.
</details>

---

### Question 19
**Who has the authority to activate a Silver Alert for a missing vulnerable senior?**

A. Any patrol supervisor
B. The Commanding Officer of the precinct of residence
C. A Captain or above from the Detective Bureau
D. The Operations Unit upon request from a precinct commander

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 207-31, ONLY a Captain or above from the Detective Bureau may activate a Silver Alert. No lower rank and no unit outside the Detective Bureau has this authority. The alert must be issued within 24 hours of determination.
</details>

---

### Question 20
**A sex crime victim presents at a hospital emergency room. How should the complaint be handled?**

A. Patrol is dispatched to the hospital to take the complaint report
B. The precinct detective squad responds to the hospital
C. The 911 dispatcher refers the complaint directly to the Special Victims Unit; patrol does NOT respond
D. The hospital contacts the precinct of occurrence, which assigns a patrol unit

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 207-33, when a sex crime victim presents at a hospital (walk-in), the 911 dispatcher refers the complaint DIRECTLY to the Special Victims Unit. Patrol is NOT directed to respond. SVU handles hospital walk-in sex crime complaints from initial response.
</details>

---

# Section 208 — Arrests

## Source
- **Patrol Guide Section:** 208
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide1.pdf
- **Priority:** High
- **Status:** Completed

## Learning Objectives
- Understand the legal standards and departmental procedures for effecting arrests
- Know the processing requirements for different categories of arrests (felony, misdemeanor, violation)
- Identify the Patrol Supervisor's responsibilities during arrest processing
- Recognize specialized arrest procedures including warrant arrests and off-duty arrests
- Master the three levels of search and authorization requirements
- Know DAT eligibility criteria and the three categories of disqualification
- Understand family offense/DV definitions, concurrent jurisdiction, and mandatory arrest provisions
- Know Miranda warning requirements and juvenile interview restrictions
- Understand lineup/showup procedures and identification requirements
- Know consular notification obligations for foreign nationals
