# Citywide Security Alert Levels

## Overview

This section covers the NYPD's tiered security alert system, which mirrors the national threat advisory framework. Sergeants must know the five alert levels by color and name, the current default level for New York City, and how changes are communicated throughout the Department.

---

## P.G. 213-18 — Citywide Security Alert Levels

### Key Points

#### Five Alert Levels (Low to High)

| Level | Color | Name | Risk Description |
|-------|-------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | **GREEN** | Low | Low risk of terrorist attack |
| 2 | **BLUE** | Guarded | General risk of terrorist attack |
| 3 | **YELLOW** | Elevated | Significant risk of terrorist attack |
| 4 | **ORANGE** | High | High risk of terrorist attack |
| 5 | **RED** | Severe | Severe risk of terrorist attack |

#### Current Status
- New York City is currently maintained at **ORANGE (High)**

#### Communication
- Changes in alert level are communicated via **FINEST Message**
- Each alert level has corresponding **security posture adjustments**

### Supervisor Responsibilities
- Know the current alert level and its implications for patrol operations
- Monitor FINEST Messages for any changes to the citywide alert level
- Brief subordinates on any alert level changes and the corresponding security posture adjustments
- Adjust patrol activities and vigilance in accordance with the current level

> **Exam Alert:** NYC is maintained at **ORANGE (High)** -- the fourth of five levels. This is the default, not an escalated condition.

> **Exam Alert:** Alert level changes are communicated via **FINEST Message** -- not roll call, not Operations Order, but FINEST Message specifically.

> **Exam Alert:** The five levels in order from lowest to highest: **GREEN (Low) -> BLUE (Guarded) -> YELLOW (Elevated) -> ORANGE (High) -> RED (Severe)**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Be prepared for exam questions that test the order of alert levels or ask which color corresponds to which threat description. A common distractor is confusing BLUE (Guarded/General risk) with GREEN (Low risk).

---

## Memory Aid — Alert Level Order

**G-B-Y-O-R** (Green, Blue, Yellow, Orange, Red)

Think: "**G**o **B**efore **Y**ou **O**verreact **R**ashly" -- levels escalate from calm (Green) to crisis (Red).

| Color | One-Word Risk | Mnemonic |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| GREEN | Low | Go |
| BLUE | General | Before |
| YELLOW | Significant | You |
| ORANGE | High | Overreact |
| RED | Severe | Rashly |

---

# Special Events Management

## Overview

This section covers the planning, execution, and management of special events, including crowd control, traffic management, and command post duties. P.G. 213-15 (Duties and Responsibilities at Special Events) and P.G. 213-17 (Traffic Notifications) are the fully extracted procedures. Sergeants assigned to special events must understand the Incident Commander hierarchy, sector management, barrier placement rules, and notification requirements.

---

## P.G. 213-11 — Policing Special Events/Crowd Control *(Source Unavailable)*

> **Note:** This procedure could not be cleanly extracted from the source PDF. The title and related information below are derived from cross-references in other procedures.

### Known Information
- Covers crowd control tactics and strategies during special events
- Complements P.G. 213-15, which defines duties and responsibilities at special events
- P.G. 213-15 references dividing event areas into sectors and maintaining emergency vehicle access lanes, suggesting 213-11 provides the tactical crowd management framework
- P.G. 213-20 (First Amendment Activities) references the Mounted Unit ensuring crowd has sufficient egress avenues and reasonable chance to disperse, which likely ties to crowd control principles in this procedure

---

## P.G. 213-12 — Apprehension Plan *(Source Unavailable)*

> **Note:** This procedure could not be cleanly extracted from the source PDF. The title and related information below are derived from cross-references in other procedures.

### Known Information
- Establishes plans for apprehending suspects during special events or mobilization scenarios
- Likely coordinates with P.G. 213-06 (Mass Arrest Processing) when large numbers of arrests are anticipated

---

## P.G. 213-14 — Command Post Duties *(Source Unavailable)*

> **Note:** This procedure could not be cleanly extracted from the source PDF. The title and related information below are derived from cross-references in other procedures.

### Known Information
- Details specific duties and roles within a command post
- Complements P.G. 213-01 (Command Post Operations) with role-specific responsibilities
- P.G. 213-15 requires a command post established at or near the event, suggesting 213-14 governs staffing and functions within that post

---

## P.G. 213-15 — Duties and Responsibilities at Special Events

### Key Points

#### Incident Commander Hierarchy
- **Single command event:** Precinct Commanding Officer is Incident Commander
- **Multi-command event:** Patrol Borough Commander is Incident Commander
- **Multi-borough event:** Chief of Patrol is Incident Commander

#### Event Area Management
- Divide event area into **sectors**
- Establish a **command post** at or near the event
- Maintain **detail roster** of all assigned personnel
- Maintain **emergency vehicle access lanes** at all times
- Identify **first aid station** location
- Establish **communications plan** with dedicated radio frequency if available

#### Barrier Placement Rules
- **100 feet** exclusion from parade route on through streets
- **25 feet** on parade route from corners
- Barrier configuration must **NOT unreasonably restrict pedestrian access**

#### Mounted Unit
- Ensure crowd has **sufficient egress avenues**
- Ensure crowd has **reasonable chance to disperse**

#### Post-Event Requirements
- **Critique report** required after the event
- Critique must include **overtime reporting**

### Supervisor Responsibilities
- Brief subordinates on event details, expected crowd size, and potential issues
- Serve as Incident Commander for single-command events (if CO is unavailable, the sergeant may be the senior MOS present)
- Establish and maintain liaison with event organizers
- Ensure barrier placement complies with distance requirements
- Monitor emergency vehicle access lanes for obstructions
- Know first aid station location and communicate it to subordinates
- Maintain communications discipline on assigned frequency

> **Exam Alert:** Incident Commander hierarchy: **Precinct CO** (single command) -> **Patrol Borough Commander** (multi-command) -> **Chief of Patrol** (multi-borough).

> **Exam Alert:** Barrier exclusion distances: **100 feet** from parade route on through streets; **25 feet** from parade route at corners.

> **Exam Alert:** Barriers must **NOT unreasonably restrict pedestrian access** -- this is a specific requirement, not discretionary.

> **Sergeant Focus:** A critique report with overtime reporting is required after every special event. The sergeant must ensure the detail roster is complete and accurate.

---

## P.G. 213-17 — Notifications Regarding Traffic Related Activities

### Key Points
- **Traffic Management Center (TMC)** must be notified of **all events** affecting traffic
- A daily **"Traffic Brief"** is compiled from these notifications
- **Operations Unit** must be notified for **unscheduled events**
- Notifications must include:
  - Location
  - Expected duration
  - Street closures
  - Alternate routes

### Supervisor Responsibilities
- Ensure timely notification to the Traffic Management Center for any event affecting traffic flow
- For unscheduled events, ensure the Operations Unit is also notified
- Provide complete information: location, duration, closures, and alternate routes

> **Exam Alert:** The **Traffic Management Center** is notified for all events affecting traffic. The **Operations Unit** is notified for **unscheduled** events specifically.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Do not assume someone else has made traffic notifications. Verify that TMC has been contacted, especially for unscheduled events where the Operations Unit must also be informed.

---

## Section Summary — Special Events

| Procedure | Title | Status | Key Takeaway |
|-----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| 213-11 | Policing Special Events/Crowd Control | Cross-reference only | Tactical crowd management |
| 213-12 | Apprehension Plan | Cross-reference only | Suspect apprehension during events |
| 213-14 | Command Post Duties | Cross-reference only | Roles within command post |
| 213-15 | Duties and Responsibilities at Special Events | **Full content** | IC hierarchy, barriers, sectors |
| 213-17 | Traffic Notifications | **Full content** | TMC and Operations Unit notifications |

---

# Key Terms — Section 213: Mobilization/Emergency Incidents

## Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| First Amendment Activity | Exercise of rights protected by the 1st Amendment: speech, assembly, petition, press | 213-20 |
| Senior Executive | Deputy chief or higher rank | 213-20 |
| Designee | Captain or above designated by a Senior Executive | 213-20 |
| De-escalation | Reducing intensity of conflict or potential conflict | 213-20 |
| Incident Commander | MOS in charge of the police response at an event or incident | 213-15, 213-20 |
| Nonviolent Participant | Person engaged in lawful First Amendment activity | 213-20 |
| Violent Behavior | Conduct involving force causing or intended to cause physical injury or property damage | 213-20 |
| Protest Liaison | Community Affairs Bureau (CAB) member assigned to communicate with demonstrators | 213-20 |
| Mass Arrest Processing Center (MAPC) | Facility activated to process large numbers of arrests during mass arrest situations | 213-06 |
| Legal Observer | Individual (NLG or NYCLU) present at demonstrations to observe police-protester interactions; granted free access through police lines | 213-20 |
| Handschu Consent Decree | Legal ruling requiring written approval from Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau for political activity investigations | 213-20 |
| FINEST Message | Department-wide communication method used to announce changes in citywide security alert levels | 213-18 |
| Traffic Brief | Daily compilation of traffic-related notifications from events affecting traffic flow | 213-17 |
| Unusual Disorder Kit | Standardized kit of supplies maintained by each command for use during unusual disorders | 213-09 |

## Critical Numbers

| Item | Value | Source |
|------|-------|--------|
| Maximum prisoners per wagon | 20 | 213-06 |
| Maximum prisoners per arresting officer | 5 | 213-06 |
| Maximum arresting officers per arrest supervisor | 4 | 213-06 |
| First Amendment arrest processing time limit (violations/misdemeanors) | 7 hours 30 minutes | 213-06 |
| Food required for prisoners held longer than | 2 hours | 213-06 |
| Barrier exclusion distance — through streets from parade route | 100 feet | 213-15 |
| Barrier exclusion distance — corners on parade route | 25 feet | 213-15 |
| Sector maps in Unusual Disorder Kit | 75 | 213-09 |
| Index cards in Unusual Disorder Kit | 200 | 213-09 |
| Complaint Report Worksheets in Unusual Disorder Kit | 200 | 213-09 |
| Aided Report Worksheets in Unusual Disorder Kit | 200 | 213-09 |
| Detail Roster sheets in Unusual Disorder Kit | 200 | 213-09 |
| Tags in Unusual Disorder Kit | 300 | 213-09 |
| Unusual Disorder Kit inspection frequency | Semiannual (January and July) | 213-09 |
| Citywide Events Planning availability | 1000-1800, Mon-Fri | 213-20 |
| NYC default security alert level | ORANGE (High) | 213-18 |
| Number of security alert levels | 5 | 213-18 |
| Number of handcuff check points | 3 | 213-20 |

## Incident Commander Hierarchy

| Event Scope | Incident Commander |
|------------|-------------------|
| Single command | Precinct Commanding Officer |
| Multi-command | Patrol Borough Commander |
| Multi-borough | Chief of Patrol |
| Tier One First Amendment | Sergeant (may serve as IC) |

## Security Alert Levels (Low to High)

| Order | Color | Name | Risk |
|-------|-------|------|------|
| 1 | GREEN | Low | Low risk |
| 2 | BLUE | Guarded | General risk |
| 3 | YELLOW | Elevated | Significant risk |
| 4 | ORANGE | High | High risk |
| 5 | RED | Severe | Severe risk |

## Legal Observer Identification

| Organization | Identifier |
|-------------|-----------|
| National Lawyers Guild (NLG) | Bright green hats |
| NYCLU | Blue hats/vests |

## Authorization Requirements

| Action | Minimum Authority Required |
|--------|--------------------------|
| Activate MAPC | Chief of Department |
| Authorize MAPC at First Amendment event | Senior Executive or designee |
| Order mass arrests at First Amendment activity | Senior Executive (deputy chief+) or designee (captain+) |
| Individual arrest for criminal conduct | Any supervisor |
| Political activity investigation (Handschu) | Written approval from Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau |

---

# Review Questions — Section 213: Mobilization/Emergency Incidents

**Instructions:** Answer each question, then expand the details to check your answer.

---

## Question 1
Who has the authority to activate a Mass Arrest Processing Center (MAPC)?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

The **Chief of Department** activates the MAPC. At First Amendment events specifically, only the **First Amendment Activity Senior Executive or designee** can authorize MAPC activation. (P.G. 213-06)

</details>

---

## Question 2
What is the maximum number of prisoners that can be transported per wagon during mass arrest processing?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**20 prisoners** per wagon. (P.G. 213-06)

</details>

---

## Question 3
What is the maximum arrest processing time for violations and misdemeanors at a First Amendment activity?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**7 hours 30 minutes.** (P.G. 213-06)

</details>

---

## Question 4
An arresting officer during mass arrest processing can be assigned no more than how many prisoners? And an arrest supervisor can be assigned no more than how many arresting officers?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

An arresting officer processes no more than **5 prisoners**. An arrest supervisor is assigned no more than **4 arresting officers**. (P.G. 213-06)

</details>

---

## Question 5
At the MAPC, prisoners must be provided food after being held for how long?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

Food must be provided if a prisoner is held **more than 2 hours**. (P.G. 213-06)

</details>

---

## Question 6
Who is the Incident Commander at a special event that spans multiple patrol boroughs?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

The **Chief of Patrol** is the Incident Commander for multi-borough events. For multi-command (single borough) events, it is the **Patrol Borough Commander**. For single command events, it is the **Precinct Commanding Officer**. (P.G. 213-15)

</details>

---

## Question 7
What are the barrier exclusion distances at a parade route?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**100 feet** exclusion from the parade route on through streets, and **25 feet** on the parade route from corners. Additionally, barrier configuration must NOT unreasonably restrict pedestrian access. (P.G. 213-15)

</details>

---

## Question 8
What is the current citywide security alert level for New York City, and how are changes communicated?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

NYC is maintained at **ORANGE (High)**, which is the fourth of five levels. Changes are communicated via **FINEST Message**. (P.G. 213-18)

</details>

---

## Question 9
List the five citywide security alert levels in order from lowest to highest, including their color and name.

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

1. **GREEN** — Low
2. **BLUE** — Guarded
3. **YELLOW** — Elevated
4. **ORANGE** — High
5. **RED** — Severe

(P.G. 213-18)

</details>

---

## Question 10
How are NLG legal observers and NYCLU legal observers identified at demonstrations?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**NLG (National Lawyers Guild)** legal observers wear **bright green hats**. **NYCLU** legal observers wear **blue hats/vests**. Both must be permitted **free access through police lines**. (P.G. 213-20)

</details>

---

## Question 11
Who has the authority to order mass arrests at a First Amendment activity? Can a sergeant order mass arrests?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

Only a **Senior Executive (deputy chief or higher)** or a **designee (captain or above)** can order mass arrests at First Amendment activities. A sergeant **cannot** order mass arrests. However, individual arrests for criminal conduct are still permitted by any supervisor. (P.G. 213-20)

</details>

---

## Question 12
What are the three required handcuff check points during First Amendment arrest processing?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

1. **Entering** the transport van
2. **Exiting** the transport van
3. **Regularly** at arrest processing

(P.G. 213-20)

</details>

---

## Question 13
Under what circumstances can body-worn cameras be activated at demonstrations?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

Body-worn cameras are **NOT activated** at demonstrations **unless** per P.G. 212-123. The mere occurrence of a protest does not authorize camera activation. (P.G. 213-20)

</details>

---

## Question 14
What is the Handschu Consent Decree, and what does it require?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

The Handschu Consent Decree requires **written approval from the Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau** for political activity investigations. It is a legal constraint limiting investigative authority at First Amendment events. (P.G. 213-20)

</details>

---

## Question 15
What role can a sergeant play in a Tier One response to a First Amendment activity?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

At a **Tier One** response, a sergeant may serve as the **Incident Commander**. Higher-tier responses require higher-ranking Incident Commanders. (P.G. 213-20)

</details>

---

## Question 16
Regarding protest liaisons from the Community Affairs Bureau (CAB), what are the uniform and assignment restrictions?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

CAB protest liaisons **must** wear uniform clearly displaying rank, name, shield, and command. They may **NOT** wear civilian attire. CAB personnel are **NOT** assigned to patrol or enforcement duties except in **exceptional circumstances**. (P.G. 213-20)

</details>

---

## Question 17
How often must the Unusual Disorder Kit be inspected, and in which months?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

The Unusual Disorder Kit requires **semiannual inspection** in **January** and **July**. (P.G. 213-09)

</details>

---

## Question 18
How many sector maps, index cards, and tags must be in an Unusual Disorder Kit?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

- **75** sector maps
- **200** index cards
- **300** tags

The kit also contains 200 Complaint Report Worksheets, 200 Aided Report Worksheets, and 200 Detail Roster sheets. (P.G. 213-09)

</details>

---

## Question 19
Which unit must be notified for all events affecting traffic, and which additional unit must be notified for unscheduled events?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

The **Traffic Management Center (TMC)** must be notified of **all** events affecting traffic. The **Operations Unit** must also be notified for **unscheduled** events. (P.G. 213-17)

</details>

---

## Question 20
What steps must be taken before enforcement action at a First Amendment activity?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

Before enforcement action at a First Amendment activity:
1. **De-escalation** attempts are required
2. **Warnings** must be given (when feasible)
3. **Sufficient resources** must be assembled before taking enforcement action
4. Only a **Senior Executive or designee** can order mass arrests

Individual arrests for criminal conduct may still be made by any supervisor without these prerequisites. (P.G. 213-20)

</details>

---

# Chapter 10 — Section 214: Quality of Life Matters

## Overview

Section 214 covers 26 Patrol Guide procedures related to quality of life enforcement, from alarm response and evictions to animal control and 311 service requests. This is **medium priority** material for the sergeant promotional exam. The procedures emphasize the supervisory role in interagency coordination, the limits of police authority in civil matters, and specific numeric thresholds that are frequently tested.

## Source

- **Patrol Guide Section:** 214
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide3.pdf
- **Priority:** Medium

## Priority Topics for Sergeant Exam

1. **311 Service Requests (214-35)** — Multiple supervisory frequencies (hourly, daily, weekly, monthly), chronic condition thresholds
2. **Unlawful Evictions (214-12)** — 30-day occupancy threshold, borough-specific return dates, NO DAT, not fingerprintable
3. **Chronic Abuser Alarm (214-08)** — 3 alarms in 3 months, three service methods, 48-hour reinstatement delay
4. **Vehicle Alarms (214-24/25)** — 3-minute observation, vehicle vs. building alarm authority, $280/$2,100 fines
5. **Care of Animals (214-33)** — Bite report in every instance, firearm use criteria, Dept of Health notifications
6. **C.U.R.E. Operations (214-39)** — 30-day notice, insufficient grounds, Chief of Patrol approval
7. **Evictions/Civil Process (214-13)** — Police role limited to peacekeeping, no property on sidewalk, no door breaking
8. **Obscenity (214-11)** — Judicial determination vs. summary enforcement distinction

## Study Tips

- Focus on numeric thresholds and timeframes — these are the most common exam question targets in Section 214
- Learn the distinction between police authority and other agency authority (DOB, TLC, city marshals) — many procedures limit MOS to peacekeeping only
- Know which supervisor level is responsible for each action (desk officer, patrol supervisor, Special Ops Lt, CO)
- Pay attention to form numbers — the exam may reference specific PD forms by number
- The vehicle alarm vs. building alarm distinction (can deactivate vehicles, cannot deactivate buildings) is a classic exam question

---

# Alarm Procedures — Chronic Abuser, Vehicle Alarm Summons, and Deactivation

## Overview

Alarm-related procedures are among the most testable quality-of-life topics for the sergeant exam. This section covers the chronic abuser alarm system for building alarms, OATH summons for vehicle alarms, and the deactivation process. Sergeants must know the specific timeframes, service methods, and the critical distinction between building and vehicle alarm authority.

---

## P.G. 214-08 — Chronic Abuser Alarm Procedures

### Key Points

- Purpose: reduce police response when **3 or more alarms** in a **3-month period** are unnecessary or unfounded
- **NOTICE OF UNNECESSARY ALARM (PD310-120)** served to a qualified person
- **No NOTICE served** if a crime was actually committed at the premises
- **10-11** = Alarm Condition signal
- **10-90N** = Notice Served disposition

### Qualified Person for Service

A qualified person includes: owner, corporate officer, manager, supervisor, or adult employee/resident.

### Service Methods

| Method | When Used |
|--------|-----------|
| Personal service | Served to qualified person on scene |
| Door service | No qualified person available — place in mailbox or under door |
| Deferred service | Cannot serve at scene — CPO serves next business day |

### Crime Prevention Officer (CPO) Duties

- Maintains master file of alarm notices
- Enters notices daily into the Alarm Board Notification System Database
- Reviews "Abuser Alarm Location Notification" (forwarded daily by IT Services Division)
- Reviews with CO to determine which locations to terminate

### Termination and Reinstatement

- **TERMINATION NOTICE (PD310-121)** served only when directed by the **precinct CO**
- Reinstatement: CO reviews the request and may require an alarm system or premises security survey
- Minimum **48-hour delay** before reinstatement takes effect

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- CO directs the issuance of TERMINATION NOTICES (PD310-121)
- CO reviews reinstatement requests and may require a security survey
- CO works with CPO to determine which locations warrant termination

> **Exam Alert:** The trigger is **3 or more** unnecessary/unfounded alarms in a **3-month period**. TERMINATION NOTICE (PD310-121) is served only when directed by the **precinct CO**. Reinstatement requires a minimum **48-hour delay**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Know the three service methods: personal, door service, and deferred (CPO serves next business day). No NOTICE is served if a crime was actually committed at the premises.

---

## P.G. 214-24 — OATH Summons for Vehicle Alarms

### Key Points

- Vehicle alarm still audible after **3 minutes**: take action
- Take all reasonable steps to disconnect **without damaging** the vehicle
- Time of violation = **end** of the 3-minute observation period
- Affix the **white cardboard (agency) copy** to the vehicle
- Issuance is **NOT mandatory** — based on discretion and/or supervisor direction
- Alarm that resets and sounds repeatedly = **one continuous action** (one summons)

### OATH Codes and Penalties

| Admin Code | Description | OATH Code | Mail-In | Maximum |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|---------|---------|
| 24-238(a) | Improper Audible Burglar Alarm | N68 | $280 | $2,100 |
| 24-238(b) | Audible Status Indicator | N71 | $280 | $2,100 |

### Towing Authority

- If unable to disconnect the alarm: place vehicle in the **Rotation Tow Program**

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- May direct MOS to issue OATH summons for vehicle alarms
- Understand that issuance is discretionary, not mandatory
- Ensure MOS observe the full 3-minute period before taking action

> **Exam Alert:** The observation period is **3 minutes**. Time of violation = **end** of the observation period. Mail-in fine is **$280**, maximum penalty is **$2,100**. An alarm that resets and re-sounds is **one continuous action**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Issuance is NOT mandatory. A repeated alarm cycle counts as one continuous action, not multiple violations. The white cardboard (agency) copy is affixed to the vehicle.

---

## P.G. 214-25 — Deactivation of Motor Vehicle Alarms

### Key Points

- Vehicle owners are required to display the **local precinct phone number** inside the vehicle
- Alarms should auto-deactivate within **10 minutes**
- MOS may attempt to disconnect within the first **10 minutes**
- Do **NOT** intentionally damage the vehicle
- Request **ESU** if difficulty entering the engine or passenger compartment
- If personal notification of the owner is not made: leave a **handwritten notification under the windshield wiper**

### Building Alarms — Critical Distinction

- MOS are **NOT permitted** to deactivate **building alarms**
- May issue a summons if a building alarm continues for **15 or more minutes**

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ensure MOS do not damage vehicles during deactivation attempts
- Direct ESU response when MOS cannot access the engine/passenger compartment
- Know the distinction: MOS CAN deactivate vehicle alarms but CANNOT deactivate building alarms

> **Exam Alert:** Vehicle alarms should auto-deactivate within **10 minutes**. MOS are **NOT permitted** to deactivate building alarms. Building alarm summons may be issued after **15 minutes**. Request **ESU** for difficult vehicle alarm access.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The critical distinction is vehicle vs. building: MOS may deactivate vehicle alarms but are NOT permitted to deactivate building alarms. For building alarms, a summons may be issued after 15 minutes of continuous sounding.

---

# Buildings and Construction — Sites, Vacant Buildings, Stop Work Orders, Security, Repairs, and DOB Warrants

## Overview

These six procedures address police responsibilities related to construction sites, vacant buildings, stop work orders, securing commercial premises, repairing damage from warrant entries, and assisting the Department of Buildings. A common theme is that MOS authority is limited — police preserve peace and address safety hazards but do not enforce civil penalties or assist in property damage.

---

## P.G. 214-16 — Construction Sites

### Key Points

- MOS: request a **supervisor** to respond
- Supervisor investigates and directs referral to another department if safety is not involved
- MOS shall **NOT** enforce Admin Code violations that carry only a civil penalty
- Emergency during non-working hours: notify the **telephone switchboard** for notification to departments concerned

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Respond to the scene and investigate
- Determine if a safety hazard exists or if the matter is a civil penalty issue
- Direct referral to the appropriate city department when safety is not involved

> **Exam Alert:** MOS shall **NOT** enforce Admin Code violations with only a civil penalty at construction sites. For emergencies during non-working hours, notify the **telephone switchboard**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The supervisor must respond, investigate, and make the determination about whether the condition involves safety or is solely a civil penalty matter.

---

## P.G. 214-17 — Vacant Buildings

### Key Points

- MOS: examine the building, notify supervisor if a hazard exists, make an Activity Log entry
- Supervisor: determine the degree of hazard, direct placement of barriers or assign officers, call **ESU** if required
- Switchboard operator: enter condition on the **HIGHWAY CONDITION RECORD** and telephone the Department of Buildings
- CO: periodically review the vacant house list and HIGHWAY CONDITION RECORD

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Determine the degree of hazard at the vacant building
- Direct barriers or post officers as needed
- Request ESU when structural or access hazards require specialized response
- CO must periodically review the vacant house list and HIGHWAY CONDITION RECORD

> **Exam Alert:** The switchboard operator enters the condition on the **HIGHWAY CONDITION RECORD** and notifies the Department of Buildings by telephone. The CO periodically reviews both the vacant house list and the HIGHWAY CONDITION RECORD.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Determine the degree of hazard and decide whether barriers, posted officers, or ESU response is appropriate.

---

## P.G. 214-18 — Stop Work Orders

### Key Points

- MOS assist the **Department of Buildings** representative in enforcing stop work orders
- Role is to preserve peace while a "Request to Appear" notice is served
- The enforcement action itself is taken by the DOB representative

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ensure MOS understand their role is limited to preserving peace
- Confirm DOB representative is present and leading the enforcement action

> **Exam Alert:** MOS role at stop work order enforcement is to **preserve peace** while the DOB representative serves the "Request to Appear" notice.

---

## P.G. 214-19 — Chains/Padlocks to Secure Commercial Premises

### Key Points

- Used for **front breaks** at commercial premises
- The **patrol supervisor** determines if the premise can be secured with a padlock and chain
- **ESU** responds, secures the premise, and attaches a **NOTIFICATION TO OWNER (MISC. 3930)**
- ESU delivers the key to the **desk officer**
- Desk officer safeguards the key and delivers it to an assigned member when the owner arrives

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Patrol supervisor makes the initial determination about whether the premise can be secured
- Ensure ESU is requested to respond, secure the premise, and attach the notification
- Verify the desk officer receives and safeguards the key

> **Exam Alert:** **ESU** secures the premises and attaches the NOTIFICATION TO OWNER (MISC. 3930). The **patrol supervisor** makes the determination to secure. ESU delivers the key to the **desk officer**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The patrol supervisor is the decision-maker on whether to secure the premises. The chain of custody for the key runs from ESU to the desk officer to the assigned member when the owner appears.

---

## P.G. 214-20 — Repair of Entrances Damaged During Forced Warrant Entry

### Key Points

- Applies when the **wrong premise** is entered during warrant execution
- Supervisor: ask the owner/tenant their preference — **self-repair or Department repair**
- CO/Duty Captain: assess the damage and notify the **Operations Unit** for the Building Maintenance Section
- MOS assigned to **secure the location until repairs are complete**
- Building Maintenance Section reports quarterly cost to the **Deputy Commissioner, Management and Budget**

### NYCHA Developments

- Repairs at NYCHA developments are handled by **NYCHA maintenance staff**
- PSA supervisor notifies NYCHA maintenance

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ask the owner/tenant whether they prefer self-repair or Department repair
- CO/Duty Captain assesses damage and contacts Operations Unit
- Assign MOS to secure the location until repairs are completed

> **Exam Alert:** This procedure applies only when the **wrong premise** is entered. The owner/tenant chooses between self-repair or Department repair. NYCHA repairs are handled by **NYCHA maintenance staff**. Building Maintenance Section reports costs **quarterly** to the Deputy Commissioner, Management and Budget.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The supervisor's first action is to ask the owner/tenant their preference for repair. An MOS must be assigned to secure the location until repairs are complete.

---

## P.G. 214-37 — DOB Access Warrants

### Key Points

- An Access Warrant is a **Court Order** allowing a DOB inspector to access premises for a safety inspection
- Desk officer: review the warrant — must be **signed by a Supreme Court judge** and **not expired**
- MOS role: **preserve peace and prevent crime ONLY**
- MOS will **NOT** assist in breaking a door or damaging property
- If access is not granted: **DOB reschedules** (not a police responsibility)

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Desk officer verifies the warrant is properly signed by a Supreme Court judge and is current
- Ensure MOS understand their role is limited to peacekeeping
- Confirm MOS do not assist in forced entry or property damage

> **Exam Alert:** The Access Warrant must be signed by a **Supreme Court judge** and must not be expired. MOS will **NOT** assist in breaking doors or damaging property. If access is denied, **DOB reschedules** — it is not a police responsibility to force entry.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The desk officer is responsible for reviewing the warrant for proper judicial signature and expiration. Police authority is strictly limited to preserving peace — DOB handles all enforcement and rescheduling.

---

# Enforcement Programs — Narcotics, Graffiti, and Establishments

## Overview

This section covers three distinct enforcement programs that target quality-of-life degradation at the community level: narcotics eviction, graffiti vandalism rewards, and multi-agency operations against problem establishments. Sergeants must understand the interagency coordination requirements, documentation standards, and the limits of police authority in each program.

---

## P.G. 214-02 — Narcotics Eviction Program

### Key Points

- Cooperative effort between NYPD and the District Attorney's Office to evict drug dealers through **civil court** proceedings
- All narcotics arrests and seizures inside locations are reviewed by the DA's Office for eviction potential
- Controlled substances must be maintained at the Police Lab or Property Clerk for a **minimum of three months**
- The DA determines whether facts substantiate a civil court action

### Evidence Standards

- **Direct evidence:** observations of sale, inculpatory statements
- **Circumstantial evidence:** 1/8 ounce or more of narcotics, cutting agents, scales, packaging materials
- **Personal use amounts do NOT support eviction**
- Activity outside homes/stores (stairwells, hallways, street) will **not** support action unless linked to interior premises

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- CO/XO/Special Ops Lt: review precinct Complaint Reports to determine if a narcotics operation is indicated
- Special Ops Lt: package information and forward to the respective county DA's Narcotics Eviction Program Coordinator
- Ensure uniformed MOS prepare the precinct COMPLAINT REPORT (PD313-152)

> **Exam Alert:** Controlled substances must be maintained at the Police Lab/Property Clerk for a minimum of **three months**. Personal use amounts do NOT support eviction proceedings.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The Special Ops Lt is responsible for packaging and forwarding information to the DA's Narcotics Eviction Program Coordinator. The CO/XO/Special Ops Lt must review all narcotics arrest reports for eviction potential.

---

## P.G. 214-03 — Graffiti Reward Program

### Key Points

- Admin Code 10-117.2 authorizes a reward of up to **$500** for information leading to the arrest of graffiti vandals
- MOS must photograph graffiti using a Department digital camera and upload via **"Graffiti Photo Upload" in OMNIFORM**
- Immediately notify the **Citywide Vandals Task Force (CVTF)** for prisoner debriefing
- Obtain a log number from CVTF and enter it in the "Notifications" section of the Complaint Report Worksheet

### Reward Eligibility

- **No law enforcement officers** eligible for rewards
- **No city employees** eligible for rewards
- Reward board is chaired by the XO, Office of Chief of Department, plus a NYC Police Foundation representative

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ensure MOS properly photograph and upload graffiti evidence through OMNIFORM
- Verify CVTF notification and log number entry on the Complaint Report Worksheet

> **Exam Alert:** The graffiti reward is up to **$500**. No law enforcement officers or city employees are eligible. Photos are uploaded via "Graffiti Photo Upload" in OMNIFORM.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Ensure MOS obtain the CVTF log number and enter it in the "Notifications" section of the Complaint Report Worksheet.

---

## P.G. 214-39 — C.U.R.E. Operations

### Key Points

- **C.U.R.E.** = Coordinating a United Resolution with Establishments
- Multi-agency operation targeting establishments causing community complaints
- Overseen by the **Mayor's Office of Nightlife**
- Coordinated by the **Legal Bureau's Civil Enforcement Unit**
- Must be approved by the **Chief of Patrol** with notice to the Mayor's Office of Nightlife
- Applies to any commercial establishment licensed or required to be licensed by the State or City

### Selection Criteria

Eligible bases for selection include:
- Complaint reports, summonses, arrests, community complaints
- 911 calls, 311 requests, general disorder, business violations
- Lack of cooperation with authorities
- Aided cases from intoxication or overdose
- Documented pattern of problems

### What is NOT Sufficient Grounds

- Refusal to attend nightlife meetings
- Property crimes beyond the establishment's control
- Crimes where security acted properly

### C.U.R.E. Notice and Timeline

- **C.U.R.E. NOTICE (PD635-121)** must be served at least **30 days** prior to the operation
- Prior notice is NOT required when it would compromise an active investigation or pose a health/safety risk
- CO determines within **30 days** of the NOTICE whether to: conduct the operation, do additional monitoring, or take no further action
- Chief of Patrol approves or disapproves through written endorsement

### Mediation Alternative

- Mayor's Office of Nightlife offers the **M.E.N.D. Program** (free mediation)
- The Department's role during C.U.R.E. operations is to assure safety of all involved

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- CO must make a determination within 30 days of serving the C.U.R.E. NOTICE
- Ensure the C.U.R.E. NOTICE (PD635-121) is properly served at least 30 days before operations
- Understand the distinction between valid and insufficient selection criteria

> **Exam Alert:** The C.U.R.E. NOTICE (PD635-121) must be served at least **30 days** prior to the operation. The CO must determine a course of action within **30 days** of the notice. The Chief of Patrol must approve the operation.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Refusal to attend nightlife meetings is NOT sufficient grounds for a C.U.R.E. operation. Know the three possible outcomes after the 30-day notice period: conduct operation, additional monitoring, or no further action.

---

# Evictions, Civil Process, and Mediation

## Overview

Eviction and civil process procedures define the boundaries of police authority in landlord-tenant disputes and civil matters. Sergeants must understand when police intervention is appropriate, the specific legal thresholds for unlawful eviction, and when mediation is the proper referral. These are highly testable topics due to the specific timeframes, return dates, and eligibility criteria.

---

## P.G. 214-12 — Unlawful Evictions

### Key Points

- Purpose: protect persons unlawfully evicted from dwelling units
- Classification: **Class A misdemeanor**, **NOT fingerprintable**

### Methods of Unlawful Eviction

- Force or violence
- Denial of essential services (heat, electricity, water)
- Removing possessions from the premises
- Removing or changing locks without providing a key
- Course of conduct interfering with the comfort of the occupant

### When the Law Applies

- Tenant has a lease, **OR**
- Occupied for **30 or more consecutive days**, **OR**
- SRO (Single Room Occupancy) tenant requesting a lease under rent stabilization

### When the Law Does NOT Apply

- Valid **Warrant of Eviction** has been issued
- **Government Order to Vacate** is in effect

### Summons Return Dates (Minimum 21 Days Out)

| Borough | Return Day |
|---------|-----------|
| Manhattan/Bronx | **Tuesday** |
| Brooklyn/Queens | **Thursday** |
| Staten Island | **First Wednesday** of the month |

### Arrest Criteria

- Arrest when violator **cannot be identified**, OR
- Arrest when violator **refuses to permit reentry**
- When arrest is necessary: bring to criminal court for **prompt arraignment — NO DAT**

### Domestic Violence Consideration

- If potential for violence between family/household members: follow DV guidelines
- Do **NOT** demand entrance in such situations

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Determine if the situation meets the criteria for unlawful eviction
- Verify the occupancy threshold (lease, 30+ days, or SRO status)
- Confirm no valid Warrant of Eviction or Government Order to Vacate exists
- Ensure proper return date on summons based on borough
- Direct arrest when violator cannot be identified or refuses reentry

> **Exam Alert:** Occupancy threshold is **30 or more consecutive days** without a lease. Summons return dates: Manhattan/Bronx = **Tuesday**, Brooklyn/Queens = **Thursday**, Staten Island = **First Wednesday**. All return dates must be at least **21 days** out. **NO DAT** when arrest is made — prompt arraignment required.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Class A misdemeanor but NOT fingerprintable. If DV potential exists between family/household members, follow DV guidelines and do NOT demand entrance. Arrest only when the violator cannot be identified OR refuses to permit reentry.

---

## P.G. 214-13 — Evictions, Repossessions, Civil Process

### Key Points

- Police role is strictly to **preserve peace and protect life/property ONLY**
- Execution of the warrant is the **sole responsibility** of the city marshal or sheriff
- MOS will **NOT** assist in breaking a door or damaging property
- City marshal/sheriff must force entry in the **least disruptive way**

### Property Removal Rules

- Marshal/sheriff or landlord are **NOT permitted** to place property on the sidewalk
- A vehicle must be on scene and property must be loaded **immediately**

### Special Circumstances

- If subject appears emotionally disturbed: comply with **P.G. 221-13**
- MOS preserve peace only — do not participate in the actual eviction process

### Documentation

- Activity Log must record: marshal's name and shield, time of entry, location, tenant name, and who is responsible for the property

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ensure MOS understand their role is limited to peacekeeping
- Verify MOS do not assist in breaking doors or damaging property
- Confirm a vehicle is present for property removal before the process begins
- Direct compliance with P.G. 221-13 if an emotionally disturbed person is encountered

> **Exam Alert:** MOS will **NOT** assist in breaking doors or damaging property. Property must **NOT** be placed on the sidewalk — a vehicle must be on scene for immediate loading. The marshal/sheriff bears sole responsibility for executing the warrant.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The police role is exclusively peacekeeping. Ensure Activity Log entries include the marshal's name/shield, time of entry, location, tenant name, and who is responsible for property.

---

## P.G. 214-40 — Mediation Referral Program

### Key Points

- Designed for **non-criminal complaints** between individuals
- Services are **confidential, voluntary, free, and expeditious**
- **REFERRAL TO MEDIATION (PD613-181)** is the required form
- Desk officer assigns a unique serial number in **YYYY-XXX-NNNN** format
- NOT to be used in lieu of enforcement action — reserved when there is **no basis** to take enforcement action

### Eligible Disputes

- Neighbor disputes (noise, driveway, property damage, pets, lifestyle)
- Roommate disputes
- Merchant disputes
- Landlord disputes

### NOT Eligible

- Criminal conduct
- Unlawful evictions (use P.G. 214-12 instead)
- Intimate partners or family members
- Child abuse or neglect
- Situations involving an Order of Protection between the parties

### Eligibility Requirements

- No threat of violence
- No criminal allegation
- No Order of Protection between parties
- Parties are not family or intimate partners
- Likely an ongoing dispute

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Special Ops Lt: weekly inspection of the precinct mediation referral file
- Ensure MOS properly complete the REFERRAL TO MEDIATION (PD613-181) with legible names, addresses, and phone numbers
- Verify mediation is not being used as a substitute for enforcement action when enforcement is warranted

> **Exam Alert:** Mediation is NOT eligible for: intimate partners/family, criminal conduct, unlawful evictions, child abuse/neglect, or parties with an Order of Protection. Serial number format is **YYYY-XXX-NNNN**. Mediation must NOT be used in lieu of enforcement action.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Special Ops Lt conducts a weekly inspection of the precinct mediation referral file. This program is reserved for situations where there is no basis to take enforcement action — it is not a substitute for proper enforcement.

---

# Miscellaneous Quality of Life — TLC Padlock, Signs, Obscenity, Refrigerators, Animals, and 311

## Overview

This section covers six diverse quality-of-life procedures. The most heavily tested topics here are care and disposition of animals (214-33) and 311 service requests (214-35). Sergeants must know the specific notification requirements, timeframes, and the limits of police authority in each area.

---

## P.G. 214-09 — TLC Padlock Enforcement

### Key Points

- MOS accompany TLC agents to padlock unlicensed livery base locations
- MOS accompany TLC for both the **initial sealing** AND the **follow-up padlocking** (10 days later)
- MOS function: **preserve peace** and ensure TLC representatives are not prevented from performing duties
- Arresting officers and complainants are provided **by TLC**
- Desk officer: assign **daily inspections** of padlocked locations

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Desk officer assigns daily inspections of padlocked locations
- Ensure MOS understand their role is peacekeeping only — TLC provides arresting officers and complainants

> **Exam Alert:** MOS accompany TLC for both the initial sealing AND the follow-up padlocking **10 days later**. TLC provides the arresting officers and complainants. The desk officer assigns **daily inspections** of padlocked locations.

---

## P.G. 214-10 — Unlawful Posting of Signs

### Key Points

- Summons for **Admin Code 10-119** (violation, not misdemeanor)
- Applies to posting on: gutter, lamppost, telephone pole, tree
- Remove the sign if possible and place it in a trash basket

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ensure MOS issue proper summons under Admin Code 10-119
- Direct removal of signs when feasible

> **Exam Alert:** Unlawful sign posting is a **violation** under Admin Code 10-119. Signs posted on gutters, lampposts, telephone poles, or trees are subject to summons.

---

## P.G. 214-11 — Obscenity/Offensive Material

### Key Points

- Governed by **PL Article 235** (Obscenity) and **PL 245.11** (Public Display of Offensive Sexual Material)
- MOS: do **NOT** take enforcement action — **immediately notify the patrol supervisor**
- Patrol supervisor: respond, determine if material meets criteria, make an Activity Log entry, and **consult Legal Bureau**
- **NO enforcement** without specific direction of the Legal Bureau

### Critical Legal Distinctions

| Offense | Classification | Enforcement |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| PL Article 235 (Obscenity) | Requires **judicial determination** that the item is obscene | No enforcement without Legal Bureau direction |
| PL 245.11 (Public Display) | **Class A misdemeanor** | Summary enforcement allowed under appropriate circumstances |

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Respond to the scene and evaluate whether the material meets legal criteria
- Consult the Legal Bureau before any enforcement action on obscenity
- Make an Activity Log entry documenting the determination
- Understand that PL 245.11 (Public Display) allows summary enforcement, while PL 235 (Obscenity) requires judicial determination

> **Exam Alert:** MOS do NOT take enforcement action — they notify the **patrol supervisor** immediately. No obscenity enforcement without **Legal Bureau** direction. Obscenity (PL 235) requires a **judicial determination**. Public Display (PL 245.11) is a **Class A misdemeanor** with summary enforcement authority.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The patrol supervisor is the decision-maker. Always consult Legal Bureau for obscenity matters. Know the distinction: PL 235 requires judicial determination; PL 245.11 allows summary enforcement.

---

## P.G. 214-32 — Discarded Refrigerators/Freezers

### Key Points

- Purpose: **prevent injury to children**
- If owner is known: direct the owner to remove the door or latch; **arrest if owner refuses**
- **ESU** responds to remove the door or latch if MOS are unable to do so

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Direct MOS to contact the owner if known
- Request ESU if MOS cannot remove the door/latch
- Authorize arrest if the owner refuses to comply

> **Exam Alert:** The purpose is to prevent injury to **children**. If the owner refuses to remove the door/latch, the owner is subject to **arrest**. Request **ESU** if MOS cannot remove the door/latch themselves.

---

## P.G. 214-33 — Care and Disposition of Animals

### Key Points

- **Dangerous animal** = menaces, threatens, attacks, or bites a person
- **DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT (PD311-152)** prepared in **every instance** — even if the animal is gone
- An animal is **NEVER** placed in the trunk of a Department vehicle
- Process offender for **DAT** if eligible

### Dangerous Dog Categories (4)

1. Unprovoked approach or menacing behavior
2. Known propensity for dangerous behavior
3. Bites without provocation
4. Used for fighting purposes

### Animal Care & Control (AC&C) Response

| Time | Resource |
|------|----------|
| Mon-Fri 0800-2000 | AC&C Rescue Units |
| 24/7 | Brooklyn AC&C Control Center |
| After hours | Contact Operations Division for on-call AC&C member |
| AC&C response >15-30 minutes | Request ESU |

### Use of Force

- Only to prevent harm or injury
- **Firearm** used ONLY to protect from **imminent physical injury** with **no opportunity to retreat**

### Desk Officer Notifications

- Notify the **Department of Health and Mental Hygiene** by phone for:
  - Rabid animal
  - Suspected rabid animal
  - Animal that bit, scratched, or abraded a human

### Agriculture & Markets Law Citations

| Section | Offense | Classification |
|---------|---------|---------------|
| 351 | Animal Fighting | Felony |
| 353 | Torture/Cruelty | Misdemeanor |
| 353-a | Aggravated Cruelty | Felony |
| 355 | Abandonment | Misdemeanor |
| 360 | Poisoning (horses, mules, cattle) | Felony |

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ensure the DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT (PD311-152) is prepared in every instance
- Desk officer must notify the Dept of Health and Mental Hygiene by phone for rabid, suspected rabid, or bite/scratch/abrasion cases
- Ensure animals are never placed in the trunk of a Department vehicle
- Evaluate use of force — firearm only when imminent physical injury and no retreat opportunity

> **Exam Alert:** The DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT (PD311-152) is prepared in **every instance**, even if the animal is gone. Animals are **NEVER** placed in the trunk of a Department vehicle. Firearms are used ONLY for **imminent physical injury** with **no opportunity to retreat**. Request ESU if AC&C response exceeds **15-30 minutes**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The desk officer must notify the Dept of Health and Mental Hygiene by phone for three situations: rabid animal, suspected rabid animal, or any animal that bit/scratched/abraded a human. Know all four dangerous dog categories and the Agriculture & Markets Law sections.

---

## P.G. 214-35 — Processing 311 Service Requests

### Key Points

- **CHRONIC 311 CONDITION:** **5 or more** requests in a **28-day period** for the same condition at the same location
- **5 or more** calls within an **8-hour period**: prioritize for **immediate response**
- MOS: notify Communications Section with **10-42 code** upon receipt, specify complaint type
- Desk officer: manually refresh the 311 system **at least every hour**

### Daily Review and Logging

- **Second Platoon Desk Officer** reviews the 311 system at **0800 daily** for the previous 24 hours
- Command Log entries required at the **beginning AND end** of each tour with the number of pending assignments

### Switchboard Operator

- Inform callers to call **311 directly** in the future

### Special Ops Lt Responsibilities

- Review deferred requests
- Design and implement response plans
- Conduct a **monthly audit** of 311 callers
- Provide a **weekly update** of the 28-day chronic conditions list posted at the desk

### CO Responsibilities

- Direct the quality of life sergeant to make **daily callbacks**
- Minimum **2 attempts** per caller

### 311 Assignment Types

Abandoned Vehicle, Animal Abuse, Blocked Driveway, Drug Activity, Encampment, Illegal Fireworks, Mass Gathering, Noise (6 types), Panhandling, Squeegee, Vending

### Administrative

- **OMAP** = designated liaison to NYC 311
- Improperly routed requests: reassign via the 311 system
- Referrals to other agencies: disposition **10-95** "Refer to Another Agency"

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Second Platoon Desk Officer: review 311 system at 0800 daily
- Desk officer: refresh 311 system at least every hour
- Command Log: entries at beginning and end of each tour
- Special Ops Lt: monthly audit, weekly chronic conditions update, review deferred requests
- CO: direct daily callbacks with minimum 2 attempts per caller

> **Exam Alert:** CHRONIC condition = **5+ requests** in **28 days**. Immediate response trigger = **5+ calls** in **8 hours**. Second Platoon Desk Officer reviews at **0800 daily**. Desk officer refreshes **at least every hour**. Special Ops Lt: **monthly** audit, **weekly** chronic conditions update. CO directs **daily** callbacks with minimum **2 attempts** per caller.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Multiple supervisory layers with different frequencies: hourly (desk officer refresh), daily (0800 review, callbacks), weekly (chronic conditions list), monthly (311 caller audit). The 10-42 code is used to notify Communications Section. Disposition 10-95 for referrals to other agencies.

---

# Vehicles and Snow — Emergency Regulations, Snow Removal, Derelict Vehicles, Expired Plates, and Parkway Removal

## Overview

These five procedures cover vehicle-related enforcement and snow emergency operations. Sergeants must know the specific timeframes for snow removal summonses, the process for handling derelict vehicles and expired registration plates, and the authority for removing vehicles from parkways and highways. Pay close attention to the 5-day timelines and the role of the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit.

---

## P.G. 214-26 — Snow Emergency Regulations

### Key Points

- Triggered by a **FINEST message** from the **DOT Commissioner**
- Summonses issued for:
  - **Traffic Reg 4-12(k)(1):** Abandoning or parking illegally on a snow street
  - **Traffic Reg 4-12(k)(2):** Operating without snow tires or chains and becoming stuck
- If a vehicle is **not immobilized**: warn the operator and direct them to leave the snow street at the nearest exit

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Monitor for FINEST messages activating snow emergency regulations
- Ensure MOS issue summonses for violations on designated snow streets
- Direct MOS to warn and redirect non-immobilized vehicles rather than immediately summonsing

> **Exam Alert:** Snow emergencies are triggered by a **FINEST message** from the **DOT Commissioner**. Vehicles not immobilized receive a warning and are directed to leave at the **nearest exit** — not an automatic summons.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The activation mechanism is the FINEST message. For vehicles that can still move, the response is a warning and direction to leave, not immediate enforcement.

---

## P.G. 214-27 — Snow Removal

### Key Points

- Summons issued for **Admin Code 16-123** if snow is not removed **four hours** after snow stops falling
- MOS must prevent the throwing of snow into cleared roadways

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ensure MOS understand the 4-hour threshold before summons issuance
- Monitor for snow being thrown into cleared roadways

> **Exam Alert:** The snow removal deadline is **four hours** after snow stops falling (Admin Code 16-123). Throwing snow into cleared roadways is also a violation.

---

## P.G. 214-29 — Derelict Vehicles with Registration Plates

### Key Points

- **Derelict** = so dilapidated, burned, or stripped as to have no value except scrap
- Derelict vehicles are only taken into custody when required as **evidence**
- The **Department of Sanitation** removes all derelict vehicles not required as evidence
- Notify the **Stolen Property Inquiry Unit** in ALL cases

### Registration Plate Removal

| Plate Type | Form |
|-----------|------|
| New York plates | REGISTRATION PLATE REMOVAL NOTICE (PD571-120) |
| Out-of-state plates | EXPIRED REGISTRATION PLATE REMOVAL PROGRAM (PD571-124) |

### Owner Notification and Timeline

- Owner notified to pick up plates within **5 days**
- If unclaimed after 5 days: forward plates and typed letterhead to DMV
- Desk officer: assign a check **5 days later**, then notify Department of Sanitation if the vehicle is still present

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ensure the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit is notified in all cases
- Desk officer assigns the 5-day follow-up check
- Desk officer notifies Department of Sanitation if the vehicle remains after the follow-up

> **Exam Alert:** Notify the **Stolen Property Inquiry Unit** in ALL derelict vehicle cases. Owner has **5 days** to pick up plates. NY plates use **PD571-120**; out-of-state plates use **PD571-124**. Department of **Sanitation** (not police) removes derelict vehicles.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The desk officer must assign the 5-day follow-up check and then contact the Department of Sanitation if the vehicle remains. Derelict vehicles are only taken into police custody when needed as evidence.

---

## P.G. 214-30 — Removal of Expired Registration Plates

### Key Points

- Applies to plates expired **more than 2 months**
- Issue a summons for unregistered vehicle
- Remove plates and follow the same process as P.G. 214-29
- **Occupied vehicle** with expired plates: remove to stationhouse, comply with **P.G. 218-12**

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ensure MOS verify plates are expired more than 2 months before taking action
- Direct proper handling of occupied vehicles with expired plates per P.G. 218-12

> **Exam Alert:** The threshold is plates expired **more than 2 months**. Occupied vehicles with expired plates are removed to the stationhouse under **P.G. 218-12**. The plate removal process follows the same steps as P.G. 214-29.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The 2-month expiration threshold is the trigger. Know the distinction between unoccupied vehicles (remove plates on scene) and occupied vehicles (remove to stationhouse per P.G. 218-12).

---

## P.G. 214-31 — Removal of Vehicles from Parkways/Highways/Expressways

### Key Points

- **Admin Code 20-511(q)** requires removal of vehicles from highways
- Advise the owner that the tow car operator is entitled to charge towing and storage fees
- Owner must appear at the precinct with the **registration certificate** for a written release
- MOS signs an **"Authorization to Tow"** if the owner refuses or the vehicle is unattended
- Notify the **Stolen Property Inquiry Unit** immediately

### Highway District Vehicles

- Vehicles with **"protective bumper guards"** may push disabled vehicles to the nearest safe parking area
- May **NOT** push vehicles to repair shops or private property

### Supervisor Responsibilities

- Ensure the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit is notified immediately
- Verify the owner is advised of towing/storage fee liability
- Confirm MOS sign the "Authorization to Tow" when the owner refuses or is absent

> **Exam Alert:** The owner must present the **registration certificate** at the precinct for a written release. Highway District vehicles with **protective bumper guards** may push to the nearest safe parking — NOT to repair shops or private property. Notify the **Stolen Property Inquiry Unit** immediately.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Two key notifications: the owner about towing fees, and the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit about the vehicle. Highway vehicles can push to safe parking only — never to repair shops or private property.

---

# Key Terms — Section 214: Quality of Life Matters

## Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| C.U.R.E. | Coordinating a United Resolution with Establishments — multi-agency operation targeting problem commercial establishments |
| M.E.N.D. Program | Free mediation offered by the Mayor's Office of Nightlife as an alternative to C.U.R.E. operations |
| Chronic 311 Condition | 5 or more 311 requests in a 28-day period for the same condition at the same location |
| Chronic Abuser Alarm | Location with 3 or more unnecessary/unfounded alarms in a 3-month period |
| Dangerous Animal | An animal that menaces, threatens, attacks, or bites a person |
| Derelict Vehicle | A vehicle so dilapidated, burned, or stripped as to have no value except scrap |
| Door Service | Method of serving alarm notice by placing in mailbox or under door when no qualified person is available |
| Deferred Service | Method where CPO serves alarm notice the next business day when service cannot be made at the scene |
| Qualified Person (alarm service) | Owner, corporate officer, manager, supervisor, or adult employee/resident |
| Unlawful Eviction | Eviction through force, denial of essential services, removing possessions, changing locks, or interfering with comfort |
| Access Warrant | Court Order signed by a Supreme Court judge allowing DOB to inspect premises |
| OMAP | Designated liaison between NYPD and NYC 311 |
| CVTF | Citywide Vandals Task Force — handles graffiti vandal debriefing |

## Critical Numbers

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| Graffiti reward maximum | $500 |
| Chronic abuser alarm trigger | 3+ alarms in 3 months |
| Alarm reinstatement delay | Minimum 48 hours |
| Vehicle alarm observation period | 3 minutes |
| Vehicle alarm auto-deactivation | 10 minutes |
| Building alarm summons threshold | 15 minutes continuous |
| OATH vehicle alarm mail-in fine | $280 |
| OATH vehicle alarm maximum fine | $2,100 |
| Unlawful eviction occupancy threshold | 30+ consecutive days |
| Unlawful eviction summons return date minimum | 21 days out |
| TLC follow-up padlocking | 10 days after initial sealing |
| Snow removal summons threshold | 4 hours after snow stops |
| Expired registration plate threshold | More than 2 months expired |
| Derelict vehicle plate pickup deadline | 5 days |
| Narcotics evidence retention (eviction) | Minimum 3 months |
| C.U.R.E. NOTICE lead time | At least 30 days before operation |
| C.U.R.E. CO determination deadline | Within 30 days of notice |
| Chronic 311 condition | 5+ requests in 28 days |
| 311 immediate response trigger | 5+ calls in 8 hours |
| 311 desk officer refresh interval | At least every hour |
| 311 daily review time | 0800 by Second Platoon Desk Officer |
| 311 callback attempts | Minimum 2 per caller |
| 311 Special Ops Lt audit frequency | Monthly |
| 311 chronic conditions list update | Weekly |

## Key Forms

| Form | Number | Used For |
|------|--------|----------|
| Complaint Report | PD313-152 | Narcotics eviction documentation |
| Notice of Unnecessary Alarm | PD310-120 | Chronic abuser alarm notification |
| Termination Notice (alarm) | PD310-121 | Terminating police alarm response |
| Registration Plate Removal Notice (NY) | PD571-120 | Derelict/expired NY plates |
| Expired Registration Plate Removal (out-of-state) | PD571-124 | Derelict/expired out-of-state plates |
| Dangerous Animal/Bite Report | PD311-152 | Every animal incident |
| C.U.R.E. Notice | PD635-121 | Notification to problem establishments |
| Referral to Mediation | PD613-181 | Non-criminal dispute referral |
| Notification to Owner | MISC. 3930 | Commercial premises secured by ESU |

## Key Radio Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 10-11 | Alarm Condition |
| 10-42 | Notify Communications Section (311 receipt) |
| 10-90N | Notice Served (alarm) |
| 10-95 | Refer to Another Agency (311 disposition) |

## Summons Return Dates — Unlawful Eviction

| Borough | Day |
|---------|-----|
| Manhattan/Bronx | Tuesday |
| Brooklyn/Queens | Thursday |
| Staten Island | First Wednesday of the month |

---

# Review Questions — Section 214: Quality of Life Matters

**Instructions:** Answer each question, then expand the details to check your answer.

## Question 1
Under the Chronic Abuser Alarm Procedures (P.G. 214-08), what triggers a location being classified as a chronic abuser, and what is the minimum delay before police response can be reinstated after termination?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

A location is classified as a chronic abuser when **3 or more alarms** in a **3-month period** are unnecessary or unfounded. After a TERMINATION NOTICE (PD310-121) is served, the minimum delay before reinstatement takes effect is **48 hours**. The CO may also require an alarm system or premises security survey before reinstatement. (P.G. 214-08)

</details>

## Question 2
A vehicle alarm has been sounding continuously. At what point can MOS take action, and what is the time of violation recorded on the OATH summons?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

MOS can take action after the vehicle alarm has been audible for **3 minutes**. The time of violation recorded on the OATH summons is the **end** of the 3-minute observation period. The mail-in fine is $280 and the maximum penalty is $2,100. If the alarm resets and sounds again, it is considered **one continuous action** — only one summons is issued. (P.G. 214-24)

</details>

## Question 3
What is the critical distinction between MOS authority to deactivate vehicle alarms versus building alarms?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

MOS **may** attempt to deactivate **vehicle alarms** (within the first 10 minutes, without intentionally damaging the vehicle). However, MOS are **NOT permitted** to deactivate **building alarms**. For building alarms that continue for **15 or more minutes**, MOS may issue a summons. ESU should be requested if MOS have difficulty accessing the engine or passenger compartment of a vehicle. (P.G. 214-25)

</details>

## Question 4
A tenant claims they were unlawfully evicted. They have no lease but have lived in the unit for five weeks. The person who changed the locks is present but refuses to let the tenant back in. What actions should the sergeant direct?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

The law applies because the tenant occupied the unit for **30 or more consecutive days** (5 weeks exceeds 30 days), even without a lease. Since the violator is present but **refuses to permit reentry**, the sergeant should direct an **arrest**. The subject must be brought to criminal court for **prompt arraignment — NO DAT** is permitted. Unlawful eviction is a **Class A misdemeanor** but is **NOT fingerprintable**. The sergeant should also confirm there is no valid Warrant of Eviction or Government Order to Vacate. (P.G. 214-12)

</details>

## Question 5
What are the summons return dates for unlawful eviction cases by borough, and what is the minimum lead time?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

All return dates must be at least **21 days** out:
- **Manhattan/Bronx:** Tuesday
- **Brooklyn/Queens:** Thursday
- **Staten Island:** First Wednesday of the month

(P.G. 214-12)

</details>

## Question 6
A dog has bitten a pedestrian but has since fled the scene. The animal is no longer present. What documentation and notifications are required?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

The **DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT (PD311-152)** must be prepared in **every instance**, even if the animal is gone. The **desk officer** must notify the **Department of Health and Mental Hygiene by phone** because the animal bit a human. If the offender (owner) is identified, process for **DAT** if eligible. The animal is never placed in the trunk of a Department vehicle. (P.G. 214-33)

</details>

## Question 7
Under P.G. 214-33, when is a member of the service authorized to use a firearm against a dangerous animal?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

A firearm may be used ONLY to protect from **imminent physical injury** when there is **no opportunity to retreat**. General use of force against animals is limited to situations necessary to prevent harm or injury. (P.G. 214-33)

</details>

## Question 8
What defines a "Chronic 311 Condition," and what triggers an immediate response? Describe the different supervisory review frequencies.

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

- **Chronic 311 Condition:** **5 or more** requests in a **28-day period** for the same condition at the same location
- **Immediate response trigger:** **5 or more** calls within an **8-hour period**

Supervisory review frequencies:
- **Hourly:** Desk officer manually refreshes the 311 system at least every hour
- **Daily:** Second Platoon Desk Officer reviews at **0800** for the previous 24 hours; CO directs daily callbacks (minimum **2 attempts** per caller)
- **Weekly:** Special Ops Lt updates the 28-day chronic conditions list
- **Monthly:** Special Ops Lt conducts an audit of 311 callers

Command Log entries are required at the **beginning and end** of each tour with the number of pending assignments. (P.G. 214-35)

</details>

## Question 9
A C.U.R.E. operation is being planned against a nightclub. The nightclub owner's only issue is refusing to attend community nightlife meetings. Is this sufficient grounds for the operation?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**No.** Refusal to attend nightlife meetings is specifically listed as **NOT sufficient grounds** for a C.U.R.E. operation. Other insufficient grounds include: property crimes beyond the establishment's control, and crimes where security acted properly. Valid grounds include complaint reports, summonses, arrests, 911/311 calls, general disorder, aided cases from intoxication/overdose, and a documented pattern of problems. (P.G. 214-39)

</details>

## Question 10
What is the required lead time for a C.U.R.E. NOTICE, and what are the three possible outcomes after the notice period?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

The **C.U.R.E. NOTICE (PD635-121)** must be served at least **30 days** prior to the operation. Prior notice is NOT required when it would compromise an active investigation or pose a health/safety risk.

Within **30 days** of the notice, the CO determines one of three outcomes:
1. **Conduct the operation**
2. **Additional monitoring**
3. **No further action**

The **Chief of Patrol** must approve or disapprove through written endorsement. The operation is coordinated by the **Legal Bureau's Civil Enforcement Unit** and overseen by the **Mayor's Office of Nightlife**. (P.G. 214-39)

</details>

## Question 11
During an eviction executed by a city marshal, the marshal asks MOS to help break down a door. Property is being placed on the sidewalk. What should the sergeant direct?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

The sergeant should direct that:
1. MOS will **NOT assist** in breaking the door or damaging property — the police role is to **preserve peace and protect life/property ONLY**
2. The marshal/sheriff or landlord is **NOT permitted** to place property on the sidewalk — a **vehicle must be on scene** and property loaded **immediately**
3. Execution of the warrant is the **sole responsibility** of the city marshal/sheriff
4. The marshal must force entry in the **least disruptive way**

The sergeant should ensure the Activity Log records: marshal's name/shield, time of entry, location, tenant name, and who is responsible for property. (P.G. 214-13)

</details>

## Question 12
MOS discover a derelict vehicle with New York plates. It is not needed as evidence. What is the step-by-step process, and what are the key timelines?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

1. Remove the registration plates
2. Prepare the **REGISTRATION PLATE REMOVAL NOTICE (PD571-120)** (for NY plates)
3. Notify the **Stolen Property Inquiry Unit** (required in ALL cases)
4. Notify the owner to pick up plates within **5 days**
5. If unclaimed after 5 days: forward plates and typed letterhead to **DMV**
6. Desk officer assigns a check **5 days later** to verify if the vehicle is still present
7. If still there: notify the **Department of Sanitation** for removal

The vehicle is NOT taken into police custody because it is not needed as evidence. The Department of Sanitation removes all derelict vehicles not required as evidence. (P.G. 214-29)

</details>

## Question 13
MOS encounter potentially obscene material at a storefront. What is the correct chain of action, and what legal distinction must the supervisor understand?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

MOS must **NOT** take enforcement action. They must **immediately notify the patrol supervisor**. The patrol supervisor must:
1. Respond to the scene
2. Determine if the material meets legal criteria
3. Make an Activity Log entry
4. **Consult the Legal Bureau**

**No enforcement** is permitted without specific direction from the Legal Bureau.

The critical legal distinction:
- **PL Article 235 (Obscenity):** Requires a **judicial determination** that the item is obscene — no summary enforcement
- **PL 245.11 (Public Display of Offensive Sexual Material):** A **Class A misdemeanor** — summary enforcement is allowed under appropriate circumstances

(P.G. 214-11)

</details>

## Question 14
What are the eligibility requirements for the Mediation Referral Program, and what types of disputes are NOT eligible?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**Eligibility requirements:**
- No threat of violence
- No criminal allegation
- No Order of Protection between the parties
- Parties are not family or intimate partners
- Likely an ongoing dispute

**NOT eligible:**
- Criminal conduct
- Unlawful evictions (handled under P.G. 214-12)
- Intimate partners or family members
- Child abuse or neglect
- Parties with an Order of Protection between them

The program uses the **REFERRAL TO MEDIATION (PD613-181)** with a serial number in **YYYY-XXX-NNNN** format. It must NOT be used in lieu of enforcement action — only when there is no basis to take enforcement action. The **Special Ops Lt** conducts a weekly inspection of the precinct mediation file. (P.G. 214-40)

</details>

## Question 15
A DOB inspector arrives at the precinct with an Access Warrant to inspect a commercial building. What must the desk officer verify, and what are the limits on MOS authority during the inspection?

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

The desk officer must verify:
1. The warrant is **signed by a Supreme Court judge**
2. The warrant is **not expired**

Limits on MOS authority:
- MOS role is to **preserve peace and prevent crime ONLY**
- MOS will **NOT** assist in breaking a door or damaging property
- If access is not granted by the occupant, **DOB reschedules** — this is not a police responsibility

The same principle applies to other civil enforcement (P.G. 214-13, 214-18): police preserve peace but do not participate in the execution of civil process. (P.G. 214-37)

</details>

---

# Section 215 — Juvenile Matters

## Chapter Overview

Section 215 of the NYPD Patrol Guide covers procedures related to juveniles, including lost and runaway children, juvenile delinquency and arrest processing, school-related incidents, child protection and advocacy, and child safety. These procedures are among the most exam-critical for the Sergeant promotion exam due to the complexity of age thresholds, Miranda requirements, notification chains, and supervisor responsibilities.

## Source

- **Patrol Guide Section:** 215
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide3.pdf
- **Priority:** High

## Learning Objectives

After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

1. Distinguish between Juvenile Delinquent, Juvenile Offender, and Adolescent Offender by age and offense type
2. Explain the Miranda warning requirements unique to juvenile interrogations, including the role of parents/guardians
3. Identify the correct notification sequence for lost children, runaway children, and child abuse cases
4. Describe supervisor responsibilities during juvenile arrests, school-related incidents, and ACS coordination
5. Recall critical age thresholds (under 11, under 16, under 18) and their procedural significance
6. Articulate school arrest protocols including School Safety Division notification and on-campus vs. off-campus enforcement
7. Explain ACS Child Protection Specialist assistance procedures and the 10-68A radio code
8. State child car seat requirements by age group

## Procedure Index

|-----------|-------|--------|
| P.G. 215-01 | Children/Minors Requiring Care and/or Shelter | Cross-reference only |
| P.G. 215-02 | Lost Child | Full text |
| P.G. 215-03 | Emergency Removals or Investigations and Reporting of Abused, Neglected or Maltreated Children | Cross-reference only |
| P.G. 215-04 | Family Court Warrant/Remand Order for Juvenile | Cross-reference only |
| P.G. 215-05 | Runaway Children | Full text |
| P.G. 215-07 | Truants | Cross-reference only |
